text: Michael McClune (born August 22, 1989) is an American former professional tennis player who reached a career-high singles ranking of No. 267 in 2010 and competed in the 2007 US Open.

The long-billed curlew (Numenius americanus) is a large North American shorebird of the family Scolopacidae. This species was also called "sicklebird" and the "candlestick bird". The species breeds in central and western North America, migrating southward and coastward for the winter.
James J. Carrigan (born April 25, 1941) is an American attorney, teacher, and former Democratic politician who served in the Massachusetts legislature from 1971 to 1973.

Nodes of Ranvier is a metalcore band from Sioux Falls, South Dakota, US, that was signed to Victory Records. The band is described as "one of Sioux Falls' most successful and popular acts ever." They have played with bands such as Bleeding Through, Every Time I Die, Bury Your Dead, Caliban, Comeback Kid, Norma Jean, Misery Signals, Extol and more across the US. The band's name refers to the myelin sheath gaps which are also called Nodes of Ranvier.

Red God (1954–1979) was a Thoroughbred race horse foaled in Kentucky who competed in England and the United States but who is best known as the sire of Blushing Groom who prominent turfman Edward L. Bowen calls one of the great international sires of the 20th century.

William Henry Seaman (November 15, 1842 – March 8, 1915) was an American lawyer and judge from Wisconsin. He served as a United States federal judge for 22 years, first in the Eastern District of Wisconsin, then on the Seventh Circuit.

Jack Wong Sue (12 September 1925 – 16 November 2009) was an Australian military veteran, author, and businessman. He served in the Royal Australian Air Force during World War II as part of Z Special Unit in Borneo and was awarded the Distinguished Conduct Medal. After the war, he opened Western Australia's first diving equipment store. He later wrote books on military and maritime history, including *Blood on Borneo* (2001). In 2006, he received the Medal of the Order of Australia for his contributions to preserving military history. He died in Perth at age 84.

Arthur is a town in Chippewa County, Wisconsin, United States. Founded in 1885 and named after President Chester A. Arthur, it had a population of 759 in 2010. The town covers 43.1 square miles (111.5 km²) and is served by the Cadott and Cornell school districts.

Ian Simmonds (born 1966) is a Welsh electronic music producer, DJ, and singer, known for his work in trip hop and collaborations with artists like Leftfield and Goldfrapp. Active since the late 1980s, he co-founded the London club "Violets" and the collective Sandals. Recording under his own name and the alias Juryman, he released influential albums such as *Last States of Nature* (1999) and *The Hill* (2000). Since 2005, he has been based in Germany, producing jazz-influenced electronic music.
Léon Spilliaert (also Leon Spilliaert; 28 July 1881 – 23 November 1946) was a Belgian symbolist painter and graphic artist.

*A Walk in the Sun* is a 1945 American war film directed by Lewis Milestone and starring Dana Andrews, Richard Conte, and John Ireland. Based on Harry Brown's 1944 novel, it follows a U.S. Army platoon during the 1943 Allied invasion of Italy as they struggle to complete a dangerous mission behind enemy lines. The film blends combat action with introspective dialogue, focusing on the psychological toll of war. Produced on a modest budget, it used repurposed military equipment for battle scenes. Though initially delayed due to World War II's end, it was critically acclaimed upon release. The film features a unique ballad-style soundtrack and was later reissued under the title *Salerno Beachhead*.

The Navy Midshipmen men's lacrosse team represents the United States Naval Academy in NCAA Division I lacrosse. Established in 1908, the team competes in the Patriot League and plays at Navy–Marine Corps Memorial Stadium in Annapolis, Maryland. Navy has won 17 national championships, including eight straight from 1960–1967, and has made 27 NCAA Tournament appearances, reaching the Final Four eight times (with runner-up finishes in 1975 and 2004). The program has also claimed five Patriot League tournament titles and eight regular-season conference championships.

The European Heritage Label is an EU initiative that designates sites of significant pan-European cultural or historical importance. Established in 2011, it replaced an earlier intergovernmental program, promoting a shared European identity through heritage recognition.

Gavan McCormack (born 1937) is an Australian historian specializing in modern East Asian history, particularly Japan. A professor at the Australian National University, he has written extensively on Japanese politics, imperialism, and environmental issues. His works include *The Emptiness of Japanese Affluence* (1996).

"Dashboard" is a song by American indie rock band Modest Mouse, released as the lead single from their 2007 album *We Were Dead Before the Ship Even Sank*. Written during the band's first collaboration with guitarist Johnny Marr, the track features improvisational lyrics by frontman Isaac Brock. The song's music video, directed by Christopher Mills, depicts a surreal nautical tale involving a giant fish, a lost hand, and an island of musical equipment, drawing inspiration from H.P. Lovecraft's themes. Released on January 16, 2007, by Epic Records, "Dashboard" was produced by Dennis Herring and includes "King Rat" as its B-side. The song marked Modest Mouse's return after a three-year hiatus since their previous single, "Ocean Breathes Salty."
London Bourne (1793–1869) was a formerly enslaved Barbadian businessman, landowner, and civil rights advocate who became one of the wealthiest merchants in Bridgetown.
Livia Orestilla (or Cornelia Orestina) was a Roman empress briefly married to Emperor Caligula in 37–38 AD after he forcibly annulled her marriage to Gaius Calpurnius Piso. Both were later exiled.
Todd Scott is the owner and publisher of National Business Review, alongside his wife, Jackie Scott.

Entrenching battalions were temporary units formed in the armies of the British Empire during the First World War. Entrenching Battalions were trained as infantry, but were primarily utilized for manual labour duties such as trench repair, wire laying, road making and assisting tunnellers, pioneers, engineers and signals. The Battalions were also used as pools of men from which drafts of replacements could be drawn by conventional infantry battalions.
Trisha Ford (born October 19, 1977) is an American softball coach and former player, currently serving as head coach at Texas A&M.
Svein Finnerud (1945–2000) was a Norwegian visual artist and jazz musician, best known for leading the avant-garde Finnerud Trio.

The Yaquina River (/jəˈkwɪnə/ yə-KWIN-ə) is a stream, 59 miles (95 km) long, on the Pacific coast of the U.S. state of Oregon. It drains an area of the Central Oregon Coast Range west of the Willamette Valley near Newport.

It rises in the mountains west of Corvallis along the county line between Benton and Lincoln counties. It flows south, then generally west, in a highly serpentine course, past Eddyville, Chitwood, Toledo, and Elk City, and enters the Pacific in Yaquina Bay, a broad estuary at Newport. When sea level was lower than today, the Yaquina River reached to Stonewall Bank, which is split by a rocky channel.

U.S. Route 20, the Corvallis–Newport highway, follows the river from Eddyville, at the confluence of the river with Little Elk Creek, to slightly downstream of Chitwood. Near the mouth of Yaquina Bay, the river passes under Yaquina Bay Bridge, which carries U.S. Route 101. Other bridges over the river include the Chitwood Covered Bridge at Chitwood.

The National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration's Pacific Marine Center and the Hatfield Marine Science Center are both along Yaquina Bay. Adjacent to the Hatfield Center is the Oregon Coast Aquarium. The Yaquina Bay Lighthouse, contained within the Yaquina Bay State Recreation Site, is on the coast near the mouth of the river.
Eric Sharp, Baron Sharp of Grimsdyke, CBE (17 August 1916 – 2 May 1994) was a British businessman and Member of the House of Lords.

The long-billed curlew (*Numenius americanus*) is a large North American shorebird known for its exceptionally long, downward-curving bill. It breeds in grasslands of west-central North America and feeds on invertebrates. Once threatened by hunting, its population has since recovered, and it is now classified as Least Concern.

Nancy Duff Campbell is an American lawyer and a founder and co-president of the National Women's Law Center. Campbell has focused on women's law and public policy issues and has participated in the development of legislative initiatives and litigation regarding women's rights, emphasizing issues affecting low‐income women, and has authored articles on women's legal issues.

HIV/AIDS in Pakistan  

HIV/AIDS in Pakistan has evolved from a low-prevalence epidemic to a concentrated one, primarily affecting people who inject drugs (PWID) and transgender sex workers. The national HIV prevalence among PWID is 27.2%, while transgender sex workers have a prevalence of 5.2%. Female sex workers and the general population remain at lower risk, though unsafe medical injections and limited surveillance pose ongoing threats.  

The epidemic emerged in the early 2000s, with the first major outbreak among injection drug users in 2003. High-risk behaviors, cultural stigma, and insufficient healthcare monitoring complicate accurate case tracking. The government's response includes prevention programs, blood screening, and antiretroviral treatment, though coverage remains low. Future challenges include improving intervention effectiveness, reducing stigma, and integrating HIV services into broader healthcare systems.
The North Bengal State Library, established in 1870, is a public library in Cooch Behar, India, housing rare manuscripts, historical documents, and over 75,000 books.
Bethuel Muzeu (born 2 February 2000), also known as Muzeu Muzeu, is a Namibian footballer who plays for National First Division club Black Leopards and the Namibia national team.
Sthenelaidas (Gr. Σθενελαίδας) was a Spartan who held the office of ephor in 432 BC, during which he played a critical role in starting the Peloponnesian War against Athens.

Pope Benedict V (Lang-la; died 4 July 965) was the bishop of Rome and ruler of the Papal States from 22 May to 23 June 964, in opposition to Leo VIII. He was overthrown by Emperor Otto I. His brief pontificate occurred at the end of a period known as the Saeculum obscurum.
Oliver County is a county located in the U.S. state of North Dakota. As of the 2020 census, the population was 1,877. Its county seat and only city is Center.

Kalopanax (*Kalopanax septemlobus*) is a deciduous tree in the family Araliaceae, native to East Asia. Growing up to 30 meters tall, it has spiny stems and large, palmately lobed leaves. Hardy to −40 °C (−40 °F), it is cultivated as an ornamental tree. In Korea, its young shoots are eaten as a blanched dish. It is considered invasive in parts of the U.S.

Air Marshal Sir Peter Edward Bairsto, KBE, CB, AFC, DL (3 August 1926 – 24 October 2017) was a Royal Air Force officer who served as Deputy Commander of Strike Command from 1981 until his retirement in 1984.

Otto Kirchheimer (IPA-de; 11 November 1905, Heilbronn – 22 November 1965, Washington, D.C.) was a German jurist of Jewish ancestry and political scientist of the Frankfurt School whose work essentially covered the state and its constitution.

Kirchheimer worked as a research analyst at the Office of Strategic Services (OSS), the forerunner of the CIA, starting in World War II and continuing to 1952.

In evolutionary genetics, mutational meltdown is a sub class of extinction vortex in which the environment and genetic predisposition mutually reinforce each other. Mutational meltdown (not to be confused with the concept of an error catastrophe) is the accumulation of harmful mutations in a small population, which leads to loss of fitness and decline of the population size, which may lead to further accumulation of deleterious mutations due to fixation by genetic drift.

A population experiencing mutational meltdown is trapped in a downward spiral and will go extinct if the phenomenon lasts for some time. Usually, the deleterious mutations would simply be selected away, but during a mutational meltdown, the number of individuals thus suffering an early death is too large relative to the overall population size so that mortality exceeds the birth rate.

Steilacoom is a town in Pierce County, Washington, United States. Incorporated in 1854, it is the oldest incorporated town in the state. Located on the Puget Sound, it was named after the Steilacoom people, a Coast Salish tribe. The town features historic landmarks, including the oldest Catholic church in Washington and the first Protestant church north of the Columbia River. As of the 2020 census, its population was 6,727.

The 2020–21 UEFA Women's Champions League knockout phase began on 9 December 2020 and concluded with the final on 16 May 2021 at Gamla Ullevi in Gothenburg. Thirty-two teams competed in a two-legged format (except the final), with Barcelona defeating Chelsea 4–0 in the final. Top scorers Jennifer Hermoso and Fran Kirby each netted six goals. The tournament was affected by COVID-19 restrictions, including a single-leg match between Vålerenga and Brøndby.

Benton Township is one of ten townships in Adair County, Missouri, United States. As of the 2010 census, its population was 19,700. It is named for Thomas Hart Benton, one of the inaugural pair of U.S. senators from Missouri.

The White Obelisk is a large stone monolith found at the ancient Assyrian settlement of Nineveh, northern Iraq. Excavated by the British archaeologist Hormuzd Rassam in 1853, it is one of only two intact obelisks to survive from the Assyrian empire, the other being the Black Obelisk of Shalmaneser III. Both are now preserved in the British Museum. The White Obelisk dates to the beginning of the Neo-Assyrian Empire and has been variously ascribed to the reigns of Ashurnasirpal I (c. 1040 BC), Tiglath-Pileser II (c. 950 BC) or Ashurnasirpal II (c. 870 BC).
Ian Moore (born 26 August 1976 in Birkenhead) is an English former professional footballer who played as a centre-forward or winger.

Lucyan David Mech (/miːtʃ/; born January 18, 1937), also known as Dave Mech, is an American biologist specializing in the study of wolves. He is a senior research scientist for the U.S. Geological Survey and an adjunct professor at the University of Minnesota. He has researched wolves since 1958 in locations including northern Minnesota, Isle Royale, Alaska, Yellowstone National Park, Ellesmere Island, and Italy.

Mech is the founder of the International Wolf Center and is the vice-chair of its board of directors. The project to create the facility, which he started in 1985, was a natural outgrowth of his wolf research as well as his ambition to educate people about the nature of wolves that they may come to respect the creature through understanding.

He has published eleven books about wolves and other wildlife, including The Wolf: The Ecology and Behavior of an Endangered Species (1970, University of Minnesota Press) and Wolves: Behavior, Ecology, and Conservation which he co-edited with Luigi Boitani (2003, University of Chicago Press). Both books remain in print as of 2017. The 1997 book The Arctic Wolf: Ten Years with the Pack received an Honorable Mention by the National Outdoor Book Award (Nature and the Environment category). His latest book with Doug Smith and Dan MacNulty is Wolves on the Hunt: the Behavior of Wolves Hunting Wild Prey.
Raymond Duchamp-Villon (1876–1918) was a French sculptor and key figure in Cubism, known for works like *The Large Horse*.
Greenwood Township is a civil township of St. Clair County in the U.S. state of Michigan. The population was 1,538 at the 2010 Census.

Diana Lee Inosanto is an American actress, director, stuntwoman, and martial artist. She also wrote and directed the film The Sensei (2008), as well as having written a children's book titled The Curious Mind of Sebastian in 2020.
Linda Gottlieb is an American producer, screenwriter, and professor.
Li Shuai (simplified Chinese: 李帅; traditional Chinese: 李帥; pinyin: Li Shuài, born 18 August 1982) is a Chinese former footballer who played as a goalkeeper.

When Hope Calls is an American family drama Western television series, created by Alfonso H. Moreno as a spin-off of *When Calls the Heart*. Set in 1916, it follows orphaned sisters Lillian and Grace as they reunite and open an orphanage in the fictional Canadian town of Brookfield. The series premiered on Hallmark Movies Now in 2019 before moving to Great American Family for its second season in 2021. Starring Morgan Kohan, Jocelyn Hudon, and Lori Loughlin, the show explores themes of family, romance, and frontier life. Filming takes place in Powassan, Ontario.

The Grand Loop Road is a historic district which encompasses the primary road system in Yellowstone National Park. Much of the 140-mile (230 km) system was originally planned by Captain Hiram M. Chittenden of the US Army Corps of Engineers in the early days of the park, when it was under military administration. The Grand Loop Road provides access to the major features of the park, including the Upper, Midway and Lower geyser basins, Mammoth Hot Springs, Tower Fall, the Grand Canyon of the Yellowstone and Yellowstone Lake.

A number of other historic districts adjoin the Grand Loop in Yellowstone, including Mammoth Hot Springs, North Entrance Road, Roosevelt Lodge, Lake Fish Hatchery and Old Faithful historic districts.

Grand Loop Rd. includes unsigned portions of US 20, US 89, US 191 and US 287, all of which traverse the park. The road was named by Harry W. Frantz in 1923, who served as the park's publicist before beginning a long career as a journalist.
Svein Finnerud (2 September 1945, Oslo – 22 June 2000) was a Norwegian jazz pianist, painter and graphic artist.
Hugues Alexandre Joseph Meunier (23 November 1758 or 1751 – 9 December 1831) was a French Army general during the French Revolutionary Wars and the Napoleonic Wars.

Pakistan's second Presidential Elections were held on 2 January 1965. The voting was to be indirect, as the President had to be elected by the 80,000 "basic democrats", who made up the Electoral College. These basic Democrats were basically democratically elected public representatives who served in the Divisional, District, Tehsil or Union councils.

There were two major parties contesting the elections: the Convention Muslim League and the Combined Opposition Parties. The Combined Opposition Parties consisted of five major opposition parties, and had a nine-point program, which included restoration of parliamentary democracy and introduction of direct elections and adult franchise. The Combined Opposition Parties had no single leadership and were therefore, unable to select a presidential candidate from amongst themselves. Hence, they chose Fatima Jinnah as their candidate who was seen as an undisputed leader due to her relationship with Muhammad Ali Jinnah. Meanwhile, the candidate for Convention Muslim League was the incumbent President Mohammed Ayub Khan.and other party Muhammad Irfan sameja

Monarchs, Rulers, Dynasties and Kingdoms of the World: An Encyclopaedic Guide to More Than 13,000 Rulers and 1,000 Dynasties from 3000 BC to the 20th Century is a non-fiction work by R.F. Tapsell, published in 1983. It is a comprehensive record of kings, queens, sultans, and emperors all in a single volume. It includes many dynasties that are rarely described except in advanced studies of individual countries or regions. The relationship of each monarch to his or her predecessor is provided which enables the reader to construct a rudimentary family tree.

Trochilinae is a subfamily of hummingbirds that includes three tribes: Lampornithini (mountain gems), Mellisugini (bees), and Trochilini (emeralds). It was redefined in 2013 based on molecular studies confirming its distinct clades within the hummingbird family.

Lashkargāh (Lang-ps; Lang-fa), historically called Bost or Boost (بست، بوست), is a city in southwestern Afghanistan and the capital of Helmand Province. It is located in Lashkargah District, where the Arghandab River merges into the Helmand River. The city has a population of 201,546 as of 2006. Lashkargah is linked by major roads with Kandahar to the east, Zaranj on the border with Iran to the west, and Farah and Herat to the north-west. It is mostly very arid and desolate. However, farming does exist around the Helmand and Arghandab rivers. Bost Airport is located on the east bank of the Helmand River, five miles north of the junction of the Helmand and Arghandab rivers. Because of the trading hubs, it is Afghanistan's second largest city in size, after Kabul and before Kandahar.

After several weeks of fighting in the Battle of Lashkargah, the city was captured by the Taliban on 13 August 2021, becoming the fourteenth provincial capital to be seized by the Taliban as part of the wider 2021 Taliban offensive.

John James Hughes (1814 – 17 June 1889) was a Welsh engineer, businessman and founder of the city of Donetsk. The village was originally named Yuzovka (Hughesovka, Lang-ru) after Hughes, ("Yuz" being a Russian approximation of Hughes), formally becoming a town in May 1917 and later, in 1924, being renamed to Stalino (Lang-uk); in 1961, the name was changed to Donetsk.

Boomplay is a music streaming service launched in 2015 in Lagos, Nigeria, primarily focusing on African and urban music. With over 70 million monthly active users, it operates across multiple platforms, including web, Android, iOS, and KaiOS. Boomplay offers subscription-based services with offline playback and has secured licensing deals with major labels like Universal Music Group, Warner Music Group, and Sony Music. It expanded into East Africa in 2016 and has offices in Nigeria, Kenya, Ghana, Tanzania, Côte d'Ivoire, and Cameroon. In 2021, Boomplay partnered with Centili to introduce data rewards for users. Its streaming data contributes to Billboard charts.

The olfactory tract is a bilateral bundle of afferent nerve fibers from the mitral and tufted cells of the olfactory bulb that connects to several target regions in the brain, including the piriform cortex, amygdala, and entorhinal cortex. It is a narrow white band, triangular on coronal section, the apex being directed upward.

Armenia Marriott Hotel Yerevan (Lang-hy), is a hotel at the central Kentron District of Yerevan, Armenia. It was opened in 1958 as Armenia Hotel, a state-owned enterprise during the Soviet period. After the collapse of the Soviet Union, the hotel was privatized in 1998 and following a major renovation; it was reopened as the Armenia Marriott Hotel Yerevan in 1999.

The hotel is located on 1 Amiryan Street, overlooking the Republic Square. The National Gallery and the government building are located in front of the hotel.

As of 2016, with 259 guestrooms, Armenia Marriott is the 2nd-largest hotel in Armenia. As of 2020, the hotel has 144 rooms in the inventory with the Ararat wing being temporarily closed.

Hopatcong State Park is a state park in the Landing section of Roxbury Township, New Jersey. Operated and maintained by the New Jersey Division of Parks and Forestry, the park consists of two parcels of land: one that encompasses Lake Hopatcong and some of its southwestern shore, and another that encompasses Lake Musconetcong about one mile to the west-southwest.

The park contains remnants of Morris Canal, which operated from the 1830s to the 1920s and was largely fed by the lake. The park is also home to the Lake Hopatcong Historical Museum, housed in the former home of the lock tender and his family.

Transbaikal, also known as Dauria, is a mountainous region in eastern Russia, stretching from Lake Baikal to the Chinese and Mongolian borders. Historically inhabited by the Daur people, it was part of the Transbaikal Oblast in Imperial Russia and later the Far Eastern Republic. The region is known for its unique flora and fauna, including the Dahurian larch, and contains protected areas like the Dauria Nature Reserve.
FOX Arena (formerly Arena) is an Australian general entertainment cable and satellite channel available on Foxtel, Austar, and Optus Television's subscription platforms.

Edith Guerrier (1870–1958) was an American librarian and social reformer. She co-founded the Saturday Evening Girls, a reading club for immigrant girls in Boston, and helped establish the Paul Revere Pottery, which provided vocational training. A pioneer in library services, she also worked with Herbert Hoover's Food Administration during World War I.

The Battle of Cabrita Point, also known Battle of Marbella, was a naval battle that took place while a combined Spanish-French force besieged Gibraltar on 10 March 1705 (21 March 1705 in the New Calendar) during the War of Spanish Succession. The battle ended in an allied victory (English, Portuguese and Dutch) which effectively ended the Franco-Spanish siege of Gibraltar.

John Landsteiner (born May 19, 1990) is an American curler and Olympic gold medalist. As the lead for John Shuster's team, he won gold at the 2018 Winter Olympics and bronze at the 2016 World Men's Championship.
Soman is a power noise/techno act from Germany. They have had several hits in the Deutsche Alternative Charts.

Climate change in the Gambia poses significant risks, including increased droughts, floods, coastal erosion, and saltwater intrusion. Agriculture, which employs 68% of the workforce, is highly vulnerable to shifting rainfall patterns. Fisheries and infrastructure also face threats from extreme weather. The Gambia has adopted a Climate Change Priority Action Plan and collaborates with international programs like the UNEP on restoration projects.

Beta Delphini (β Delphini, also named Rotanev) is a binary star system in the constellation Delphinus. It consists of two F-type stars—a primary giant and a secondary subgiant—orbiting each other every 26.66 years. Located about 101 light-years away, the system was discovered in 1873 and later named after Niccolò Cacciatore (Latinized as *Nicolaus Venator*), whose reversed name forms "Rotanev."

Emerich Vogl (12 August 1905 – 29 October 1971) was a Romanian footballer and manager. A versatile defender and midfielder, he played for Chinezul Timișoara and Juventus București, winning multiple league titles. He represented Romania at the 1930 and 1934 FIFA World Cups. After retiring, he managed Juventus București and the Romania national team, later serving as a consultant for Rapid București and the national team.

The USS Arizona (1858) was a sidewheel steamer that served as a Confederate blockade runner (CSS *Caroline*) before being captured in 1862. Commissioned into the U.S. Navy in 1863, it participated in Union operations along the Mississippi and Red Rivers during the Civil War. The ship was destroyed by fire in 1865.

W. & J. Sloane was a luxury furniture and rug store in New York City that catered to the prominent, including the White House and the Breakers, and wealthy, including the Rockefeller, Whitney, and Vanderbilt families.

After a long period of prosperity and prominence, the firm went through a short period of decline and was forced to file for bankruptcy in 1985.

Jitendra Malik is an Indian-American academic who is the Arthur J. Chick Professor of Electrical Engineering and Computer Sciences at the University of California, Berkeley. He is known for his research in computer vision.

Nintendo PowerFest '94 was a nationwide gaming competition held by Nintendo in 1994, featuring *Super Mario Bros.: The Lost Levels*, *Super Mario Kart*, and *Major League Baseball*. Regional winners advanced to the finals in San Diego, where Mike Iarossi was crowned champion. Special cartridges were used, with only two known to survive today. The event was a precursor to the *Nintendo World Championships* series.

Final Account is a 2020 documentary film directed by Luke Holland, featuring interviews with former Nazis and German civilians about their roles in the Holocaust. Produced over a decade, the film explores themes of complicity and memory. It premiered at the Venice Film Festival and received critical acclaim, with a 93% approval rating on Rotten Tomatoes. Holland, whose family was affected by the Holocaust, died shortly after completing the film.

Blaže Koneski (Lang-mk; 19 December 1921 – 7 December 1993) was a Macedonian poet, writer, literary translator, and linguistic scholar.

His major contribution was to the codification of standard Macedonian. He is the key figure who shaped Macedonian literature and intellectual life in the country however he has also been accused of serbianizing the Macedonian standard language.

Yaser Abdel Said (Lang-ar; born January 27, 1957) is an Egyptian-American former taxi driver and convicted murderer. For 12 years, Said evaded arrest for the January 1, 2008, fatal shootings of his two daughters, Amina (18) and Sarah (17). Their bodies were found in his abandoned taxi cab in Irving, Texas, on the property of the Omni Mandalay Hotel (now the Omni Las Colinas Hotel).

Said disappeared after the killings. He also remained a fugitive from law enforcement for 12 years, with six of those years being on the FBI Ten Most Wanted Fugitives List for the fatal shootings.

Said, who was very controlling over his family, felt that his daughters Amina and Sarah dishonored the family. On January 1, 2008, he lured them into his taxi cab on the pretense of taking them out to eat, where instead, he fatally shot them.

Said was captured on August 26, 2020 in the town of Justin, Texas. His son, Islam, and Said's brother, Yassein, were both arrested in Euless, Texas, for aiding a fugitive. Said was taken into federal custody by the FBI, and was then transferred to Dallas County where he was held until his trial, which commenced in August 2022. Said was found guilty on August 9, after a week-long trial.

David Production Inc. (Japanese: 株式会社デイヴィッドプロダクション, Hepburn: Kabushiki-gaisha Deividdo Purodakushon) is a Japanese animation studio founded in September 2007 and is based in Nishitōkyō, Tokyo. Notable works from the studio include JoJo's Bizarre Adventure, Cells at Work!, and Fire Force.

Hirebenakal or Hirébeṇakal or Hirébeṇakallu (ಹಿರೇಬೆಣಕಲ್ಲು in Kannada) is a megalithic site in the state of Karnataka, India. It is among the few megalithic sites in India that can be dated to the 800 BCE to 200 BCE period. The site is located in the Koppal district, some 10 kilometres (6.2 mi) west of the town of Gangavati and some 35 kilometres (22 mi) from Hospet city. It contains roughly 400 megalithic funerary monuments, that have been dated to the transition period between Neolithic period and the Iron Age. Known locally (in the Kannada language) as eḷu guḍḍagaḷu (or 'the seven hills'), their specific name is moryar guḍḍa (or 'the hill of the moryas"). Hirebenakal is reported to be the largest necropolis among the 2000 odd megalithic sites found in South India, most of them in the state of Karnataka. Since 1955, it has been under the management of the Dharwad circle of the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI). On May 19, 2021, it was proposed that Hirebenakal be made a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

EC KAC is an Austrian professional ice hockey team based in Klagenfurt, Carinthia. Founded in 1909, it is the most successful club in Austrian hockey history, with 32 national championships. The team plays in the ICE Hockey League at Stadthalle Klagenfurt (capacity: 5,500). EC KAC has also competed in European competitions, reaching the European Cup final in 1970. It remains one of Austria's top hockey clubs.
Ruth Cameron is an American record producer and jazz vocalist.

The 2020–21 UEFA Women's Champions League knockout phase began on 9 December 2020 with the round of 32 and ended with the final on 16 May 2021 at the Gamla Ullevi in Gothenburg, Sweden, to decide the champions of the 2020–21 UEFA Women's Champions League. A total of 32 teams competed in the knockout phase.

Cabot Tower is a Gothic Revival tower in St. John's, Newfoundland and Labrador, Canada. Completed in 1900, it commemorates both Queen Victoria's Diamond Jubilee and the 400th anniversary of John Cabot's voyage to Newfoundland. Designed by architect William Howe Greene, the 50-foot (15 m) tower served as a flag signaling station until 1958 and later housed a Marconi wireless station. It is now a historic landmark, offering panoramic views of the city and Atlantic Ocean. The tower remains one of St. John's most recognizable symbols.

"Tight Connection to My Heart (Has Anybody Seen My Love)" is a song by Bob Dylan that was released on his 1985 album Empire Burlesque. As a single, it was a Top 40 Hit in New Zealand and Belgium. An earlier version of the song, entitled "Someone's Got a Hold of My Heart", was recorded for Dylan's 1983 LP Infidels, but was not included on that album; it later appeared on The Bootleg Series Volumes 1–3 (Rare & Unreleased) 1961–1991.
The 1960 Spanish Guinea by-election indirectly elected six members to Spain's Cortes Españolas.
The WAGR M class were diesel locomotives operated by Western Australian Government Railways, primarily used for shunting. Some were later sold for sugar cane operations in Queensland.

Shaheed Latif (11 June 1913 – 16 April 1967) was an Indian film director, screenwriter, and producer. He directed *Ziddi* (1948), launching Dev Anand's career, and collaborated frequently with his wife, writer Ismat Chughtai. He worked at Bombay Talkies early in his career.
A by-election to the Spanish Cortes Españolas was held in Spanish Guinea in 1960.

Radio Dabanga is an independent Sudanese radio and television station broadcasting news and programs in Sudanese Arabic, primarily targeting audiences in Sudan. Founded on December 1, 2008, and operated by the Dutch NGO Free Press Unlimited, it initially focused on conflict-affected regions like Darfur but has since expanded to cover national issues. The station broadcasts daily via shortwave radio (morning and evening) and satellite TV (24/7), reaching millions of listeners and viewers. Its programs include news bulletins, reunification segments like *Lost and Found*, cultural shows such as *Melodies from Darfur*, and justice-focused content like *Fi Al Mizan*.  

Radio Dabanga operates from Amsterdam, relying on field reporters and audience contributions via phone and WhatsApp. Despite facing censorship attempts by the Sudanese government—including satellite broadcast bans—it remains a key source of independent journalism. The station also maintains a strong online presence through its website, social media, and SoundCloud.

The First Congregational Church and Parsonage is a historic church complex at 23 Pepperrell Road (Maine State Route 103) in the Kittery Point section of Kittery, Maine. Built in 1730 for a congregation first organized in 1653, the church is the oldest in Kittery, and one of the oldest in the state of Maine. It is accompanied by a parsonage house, built in 1729, and a small cemetery, established in 1733. The buildings were listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1978; the cemetery was added to the listing in 1997.

James McCulloch (4 June 1953 – 27 September 1979) was a Scottish musician best known for playing lead guitar and bass, as a member of Paul McCartney's band Wings from 1974 to 1977. McCulloch was a member of the Glasgow psychedelic band One in a Million (formerly known as the Jaygars), Thunderclap Newman, and Stone the Crows.

McCulloch also made appearances on many albums, including John Entwistle's Whistle Rymes in 1972, as lead guitarist playing alongside Peter Frampton on "Apron Strings" and "I Feel Better". McCulloch also played guitar on Roger Daltrey's album One of the Boys which was released in 1977. McCulloch was a friend of the Who and a member of the band Thunderclap Newman, which was created and produced by his mentor Pete Townshend. At age 11, he was also a protégé of the Shadows' Hank Marvin. His brother is drummer Jack McCulloch.

Typhoon Hagupit, known in the Philippines as Typhoon Dindo, was a Category 1 typhoon that struck East Asia in August 2020. Forming on July 30 in the Philippine Sea, it intensified into a typhoon before making landfall in Zhejiang, China, on August 3 with winds of 140 km/h (85 mph). The storm brought heavy rainfall, flooding, and strong winds, causing 17 fatalities and $411 million in damage, primarily in China and South Korea.  

In China, over 200,000 people were evacuated, and widespread power outages affected 830,000 customers. A woman died in Yuhuan after falling from a broken window, while flooding and landslides in South Korea killed 15 people and left 11 missing. The storm also impacted Taiwan and the Ryukyu Islands before transitioning into an extratropical cyclone and dissipating near Alaska on August 14.  

Hagupit was part of the 2020 Pacific typhoon season, contributing to one of the most active years on record.

The 2012 West Oxfordshire District Council election was held to elect 17 of the 49 councillors. The Conservatives retained control despite losing three seats to Labour, reducing their majority to 41 seats. Labour and the Liberal Democrats each held four seats. Turnout averaged 34.77%, and three subsequent by-elections were held in 2013.

Titus Haterius Nepos was a Roman equestrian official who served as prefect of Egypt (120–124 AD). His career included military and administrative roles, including census official in Britain, procurator of Armenia, and prefect of the vigiles. He is known for visiting the Colossi of Memnon in 121. His identification is based on an inscription from Fulginiae and a letter from Vindolanda.

The 2017 NHL Expansion Draft was held on June 21, 2017, to select players for the newly formed Vegas Golden Knights. Each existing NHL team could protect either seven forwards, three defensemen, and one goaltender, or eight skaters and one goaltender. The draft followed strict exposure rules to ensure competitive balance, with Vegas required to select at least 14 forwards, nine defensemen, and three goaltenders.

Substance Church is a nondenominational, Evangelical Christian, megachurch in Minneapolis–Saint Paul, Minnesota, United States. In 2010, Outreach Magazine ranked the church as the 21st fastest growing congregation in the United States (8th in terms of growth percentages). Because the church has integrated ultra-contemporary worship, cell church, and cafe church methods into a singular megachurch setting, Substance is often cited as being "trend-setting".

Beta Delphini (β Delphini, abbreviated Beta Del, β Del) is a binary star in the constellation of Delphinus. It is the brightest star in Delphinus.

The two components of the system are designated Beta Delphini A (officially named Rotanev /ˈroʊtənɛv/, which is historically the name of the system) and B.

World Wide Adventures was a series of live-action travelogue short films distributed by Warner Bros. in the late 1950s alongside their animated cartoons. Primarily independent productions, a few were supervised in-house by Cedric Francis and William L. Hendricks. Though largely forgotten, some, like *Kingdom of the Saguenay* and *See Holland Before It Gets Too Big*, later aired on Turner Classic Movies. The series remains poorly documented.

St John's Rectory is a Grade II* listed Georgian building in Chester, England, built around 1750 as the rectory for St John's Church. It features a symmetrical brick façade with stone dressings and a notable Gibbs-surround entrance.

Jimmy McCulloch (4 June 1953 – 27 September 1979) was a Scottish guitarist best known for his work with Wings and Thunderclap Newman. Born in Dumbarton, he began playing guitar at age 11 and rose to fame in 1969 as the teenage lead guitarist of Thunderclap Newman, whose single "Something in the Air" reached No. 1 in the UK. After stints with Stone the Crows and session work for artists like John Entwistle, he joined Paul McCartney & Wings in 1974, contributing to albums such as *Venus and Mars* and *Wings at the Speed of Sound*. He later played with Small Faces and Wild Horses before his death at 26 from a drug and alcohol-related heart failure. McCulloch was known for his blues-infused guitar style, often using Gibson SG and Les Paul models.

Alice – A Fight for Life is a 1982 British documentary film produced and narrated by investigative journalist John Willis for Yorkshire Television. Directed by Willis, the film examines the dangers of asbestos exposure, focusing on the case of Alice Jefferson, a factory worker suffering from mesothelioma. It critiques the asbestos industry's influence on workplace safety regulations and highlights corporate negligence. The documentary builds on Willis's earlier investigative work, including *Johnny Go Home* (1975) and *Rampton - The Secret Hospital* (1979). Broadcast on ITV, the film contributed to public awareness and policy debates on asbestos-related diseases. Running 94 minutes, it remains a significant work in British documentary filmmaking.

Rainbow War is a 1985 Oscar-nominated 19-minute animated/live action short film created for Expo 86, the 1986 World's Fair in Vancouver, British Columbia, Canada. It was directed by Bob Rogers and cinematography was done by Reed Smoot.

Notable cast members include Ryan Stiles and Colin Mochrie of Whose Line is it Anyway fame.

The Philippine Center is a government-owned building in New York City that serves as a hub for Philippine cultural and diplomatic activities. Established in 1973 under Presidential Decree No. 188, it houses offices of the Philippine government and features the Kalayaan Hall, an art gallery, and a collection of works by Filipino artists. Located at 556 Fifth Avenue, the building was originally designed by Carrere & Hastings in 1912. A second Philippine Center was later established in San Francisco. The center serves as a cultural landmark for Filipinos abroad, often described as a "home away from home."

Entrenching battalions were temporary military units formed by the British, Canadian, and New Zealand armies during World War I to construct and improve defensive fortifications. The British Army first established them in 1916, disbanding and later reviving them in 1918 to bolster defenses ahead of the German spring offensive. Canadian and New Zealand forces also formed similar units, which were later dissolved as manpower needs shifted. These battalions played a key role in trench warfare before being absorbed into regular infantry units.

Qi Xin (simplified Chinese: 齐心; traditional Chinese: 齊心; born November 3, 1926) is a Chinese author and member of the Chinese Communist Party (CCP), who wrote various articles on her husband, Chinese communist revolutionary Xi Zhongxun. She is the mother of Xi Jinping, current General Secretary of the Chinese Communist Party, also known as the paramount leader.

Stanstead is a village and civil parish in Suffolk, England. The name Stanstead comes from the Old English for "Stony place". Located off the B1066, it is around 4 miles (6 km) from Sudbury, and is part of Babergh district. It is about 2 kilometres (1 mi) from Glemsford, 12 miles (19 km) from Hadleigh, and 4 miles (6 km) from Lavenham.
Judith Magdolna Laqueur-Révész (15 July 1915 – 17 April 2018) was a Hungarian-Dutch potter and sculptor.

"You're a Big Girl Now" is a 1975 folk rock song by Bob Dylan from his album *Blood on the Tracks*. A melancholic breakup ballad, it features Dylan's emotive vocals and sparse instrumentation. Critics have praised its raw expression of heartbreak, with *Rolling Stone* ranking it among Dylan's greatest songs. The track was recorded in Minneapolis with a small band, and alternate versions were later released on compilations. Dylan performed it live over 200 times.

Ronald Horvath (born in Scranton, Pennsylvania) is an American geographer known for his work in urban studies, transport geography, and social justice activism, including involvement in the Detroit Geographical Expedition. He taught at several universities before retiring from the University of Sydney.  

*(26 words exactly)*
Kate Addo (born 23 July 1972) is a Ghanaian journalist, broadcaster and public relations practitioner. She is currently the Director of Public Affairs of the Parliament of Ghana.

Cardea or Carna was a Roman goddess associated with door hinges (*cardo*) and thresholds, often linked with minor deities like Forculus and Limentinus. She played a role in rituals marking sacred boundaries and was sometimes conflated with Carna, a goddess of health and protector against vampiric *striges*. Ovid’s *Fasti* recounts her mythic rape by Janus, who granted her power over doorways and gave her the whitethorn to ward off evil. Her festival, the *Kalendae fabariae*, involved offerings of beans and lard. Modern interpretations vary, seeing her as a chthonic, lunar, or digestive deity.
The alternative Christmas message is a short televised speech broadcast by Channel 4 since 1993, often featuring a controversial figure as a counterpoint to the British monarch's Christmas message.
Lev Dodin (born 1944) is a Russian theater director who has led the Maly Drama Theatre since 1982.
Birdmonster is an American rock band based in San Francisco, California.

The 2017 NHL Expansion Draft was an expansion draft conducted by the National Hockey League on June 18–20, 2017 to fill the roster of the league's expansion team for the 2017–18 season, the Vegas Golden Knights. The team's selections were announced on June 21 during the NHL Awards ceremony at T-Mobile Arena.

The Battle of Cabrita Point (21 March 1705) was a naval engagement during the War of the Spanish Succession. A combined English, Portuguese, and Dutch fleet under Admiral John Leake defeated a French squadron near Gibraltar, capturing three ships and destroying two. The victory secured Allied control of Gibraltar, forcing the French and Spanish to abandon their siege and withdraw.

Slaughter Studios is a 2002 American horror film directed by Brian Katkin and starring Nicolas Read, Amy Shelton-White, and Tara Killian. The film follows a group of film students who break into an abandoned horror movie studio to shoot a low-budget feature in one night, only to be hunted by a mysterious killer. The plot centers on Steve (Peter Stanovich), an arrogant director obsessed with the studio's dark past, who ignores mounting disappearances among his cast and crew while pushing to complete his film. As the night progresses, the killer—revealed to be a vengeful homeless man—murders the students one by one, with only a few survivors escaping. The film originally included a meta-ending revealing the events as part of a fictional production, but this was cut from the final release. Distributed by New Concorde, *Slaughter Studios* blends slasher tropes with dark humor and a self-referential take on low-budget filmmaking.

The first USS Arizona was an iron-hulled, side-wheel merchant steamship. Seized by the Confederate States of America in 1862 during the American Civil War, she was captured later the same year by the United States Navy.

A debug port is a hardware or software interface used to test, diagnose, and program microprocessors and microcontrollers. On complex microprocessors, debug ports assist in troubleshooting intricate circuitry. Microcontrollers, particularly system-on-a-chip (SoC) designs, often rely on debug ports for initial configuration, clock tree setup, and firmware loading. Some modern microcontrollers integrate USB Device Firmware Upgrade (DFU) in factory ROM, allowing recovery from faulty firmware without specialized hardware. However, DFU lacks the reliability of dedicated hardware debug ports like JTAG, as it depends on functional software for debugging. Debug ports remain essential for embedded system development and repair.
Eric Sharp, Baron Sharp of Grimsdyke (17 August 1916 – 2 May 1994) was a British businessman who chaired Cable & Wireless plc and was made a life peer in 1989.

Birna Arnbjörnsdóttir (born 1952) is an Icelandic linguist specializing in second-language acquisition. A professor at the University of Iceland, she researches heritage languages, bilingualism, and English influence on Icelandic. She also developed *Icelandic Online*, a free digital language course.

Maureen Sullivan (born 1952) is an American librarian and educator who served as president of the American Library Association (2012–2013) and the Association of College and Research Libraries (1998–1999).

Menodora is a genus of flowering plants in the olive family (Oleaceae), comprising species with bright yellow (or white) scented flowers and bilobed fruits. Native to arid regions of the Americas and Africa, it exhibits a disjunct distribution, likely due to long-distance dispersal.

Muslim Community Radio (MCR) is an Islamic radio station broadcasting from the London Muslim Centre in Tower Hamlets. Launched in 1998, it airs in English and Bengali on 1143 AM, offering religious and cultural programming, including Qur'anic classes, talk shows, and Ramadan broadcasts. Owned by Islamic Forum Europe, it operates with volunteer presenters and community support.

Baaji (Urdu: باجی) is a 2019 Pakistani drama film directed by Saqib Malik and written by Irfan Ahmed Urfi. Produced by Page 33 Films and distributed by ARY Films, it stars Meera, Osman Khalid Butt, Amna Ilyas, Mohsin Abbas Haider, Ali Kazmi, and Nayyar Ejaz. The film follows the struggles of a fading Lollywood actress and explores themes of ambition, rivalry, and the entertainment industry.  

Released on June 28, 2019, *Baaji* generated significant buzz due to its controversial song *Gangster Guriya* and received a positive critical response, with praise for Meera and Ilyas' performances. It earned ₹12 crore domestically, doubling its ₹6 crore budget, and was declared a commercial success. The film later streamed on Amazon Prime Video and had a television premiere on ARY Digital. Critics, including Galaxy Lollywood, lauded its portrayal of Pakistan's film industry, calling it a vibrant yet dark depiction of Lollywood's legacy.

R (Corner House Research) v Director of the Serious Fraud Office [2008] UKHL 60 was a UK case where the House of Lords upheld the Serious Fraud Office's decision to halt a bribery investigation into BAE Systems after Saudi Arabia threatened national security.
Henry Monsang Tesong (born 28 February 1994) is an Indian professional footballer who plays as a defender for Manipur State League side Sagolband United.

The Habbari (Lang-ar, Sindhi: حباري خاندان) were an Arab dynasty that ruled much of Greater Sindh, as a semi-independent emirate from 854 to 1024. Beginning with the rule of 'Umar bin Abdul Aziz al-Habbari in 854 CE, the region became semi-independent from the Abbasid Caliphate in 861, while continuing to nominally pledge allegiance to the Abbasid Caliph in Baghdad. The Habbari ascension marked the end of a period of direct rule of Sindh by the Umayyad and Abbasid Caliphates, which had begun in 711 CE. The Habbaris were based in the city of Mansura, and ruled central and southern Sindh south of Aror, near the modern-day metropolis of Sukkur. The Habbaris ruled Sindh until they were defeated by Sultan Mahmud Ghaznavi in 1026, who then went on to destroy the old Habbari capital of Mansura, and annex the region to the Ghaznavid Empire, thereby ending Arab rule of Sindh.

HIV is recognized as a health concern in Pakistan with the number of cases growing. Moderately high drug use and lack of acceptance that non-marital sex is common in the society have allowed the HIV epidemic to take hold in Pakistan, mainly among injecting drug users (IDU), male, female and transvestite sex workers (MSW, FSW and TSW) as well as the repatriated migrant workers. HIV infection can lead to AIDS that may become a major health issue.

The National AIDS Programme's latest figures show that by the mid-2000s, the number of HIV cases had increased to approximately 0,102 million. This number was estimated little over 4,000, as the HIV cases reported since 1986. The UN and government estimated the number of HIV/AIDS cases around 97,000 ranging from a lowest estimate of 46,000 to a highest estimate of 210,000.

More realistic estimates that are based on actual surveillance figures, however, suggest that this number may be closer to 40,000–45,000. The overall prevalence of HIV infection in adults aged 15 to 49 is 0.1%. (0.05% if one accepts the lower estimates). Officials say that the majority of cases go unreported due to social taboos about sex and victims’ fears of discrimination. The other reason for all the cases not being reported is that HIV is a disease either not systematically tested for or reported in the routine surveillance system.

Clare Maguire (born 15 September 1988) is an English singer-songwriter known for her soulful vocals and blending of pop, blues, and electronic influences. Rising to prominence in 2010 with her debut album *Light After Dark*, which peaked at No. 7 on the UK Albums Chart, she gained recognition for singles like "Ain't Nobody" and "The Last Dance." Maguire has collaborated with artists such as Mike Skinner (The Streets) and Chase & Status. After a period of independent releases, including the *Don't Mess Me Around* EP (2015), she returned with her second album, *Stranger Things Have Happened* (2016). Her music has been praised for its emotional depth and genre versatility, drawing comparisons to artists like Johnny Cash and Ella Fitzgerald.

The 1923 Chicago mayoral election was held on April 3, 1923, resulting in the election of Democratic nominee William E. Dever over Republican Arthur C. Lueder and Socialist William A. Cunnea. Dever won by a large margin, capitalizing on Republican divisions and dissatisfaction with outgoing mayor William H. Thompson. Voter turnout was 78%, the second-lowest in over two decades.

Paddleton is a 2019 American comedy-drama film, directed by Alex Lehmann, from a script by Lehmann and Mark Duplass. The film stars Duplass and Ray Romano.

The film had its world premiere at the Sundance Film Festival on February 1, 2019. It was released on February 22, 2019, by Netflix to generally favorable reviews from critics.

2002 Euler is a background asteroid in the inner main belt, discovered on 29 August 1973 by Tamara Smirnova at the Crimean Astrophysical Observatory. Named after Swiss mathematician Leonhard Euler, it orbits the Sun every 3.76 years. With a diameter of approximately 17–20 km and a low albedo, it is classified as an S-type asteroid.

W. & J. Sloane was a luxury home furnishings retailer founded in 1843 by William Sloane in New York City. Originally an importer of oriental rugs, it expanded into furniture and interior decorating, serving elite clients and furnishing landmarks like the White House. After decades as a taste-setter, it was acquired by City Stores Company in 1955, declined in quality, and filed for bankruptcy in 1985.

Ultrasound-modulated optical tomography (UOT) is an imaging technique that combines ultrasound and laser light to visualize soft tissue. It modulates light using ultrasonic waves to detect variations in optical properties, enabling deeper tissue penetration than traditional optical methods. UOT relies on scattered photons and can generate images in diffusive biological tissues. The technique was first proposed in 2013 for virus detection.

Waldorf-Astoria Hotel and Residence Tower Chicago, was a planned hotel and condominium skyscraper that would have been 1,265 feet (386 m) tall. The Prime Group was in charge of the project, which was proposed at a cost of $610 million. The building would have been located one block from the Chicago River, and north of the Sheraton Chicago in the Streeterville neighborhood of the Near North Side community area of Chicago. The building's developers, the Fordham Company, were the initial developers of the now-cancelled Chicago Spire.

The building would have been the third tallest building in Chicago as only the Willis Tower and the Trump International Hotel and Tower are taller than 1,200 feet (366 m). It would have been among the tallest buildings in the world, since only 25 buildings in the world currently exceed 1,200 feet (366 m).

Early in 2012 the approval process began for a much smaller mixed-use tower on the site.

The Canajoharie Creek (/ˌkænədʒəˈhɛəri/) is a river that flows into the Mohawk River in the Village of Canajoharie in the U.S. State of New York. The name "Canajoharie" is a Mohawk language term meaning "the pot that washes itself", referring to the "Canajoharie Boiling Pot", a 20-foot (6.1 m) wide and 10-foot (3.0 m) deep pothole in the Canajoharie Creek, just south of the village of Canajoharie. Bowmans Creek is one main tributary that enters the creek east of the Hamlet of Sprout Brook. The other main tributary is Brimstone Creek which enters the creek north-northwest of the Village of Ames.
John Graham is a Canadian former Native American activist. He is best known for being the person who shot and killed fellow American Indian Movement activist Anna Mae Aquash.

Nodes of Ranvier was an American metalcore band from Sioux Falls, South Dakota, active from 2000 to 2008 before reuniting in 2017. Known for their Christian metal influences, they released three albums on Facedown Records before signing with Victory Records for their final album, *Defined by Struggle* (2007). Despite lineup changes—including 18 members over their career—the band maintained a dedicated following. After disbanding in 2007, they reunited for Facedown Fest in 2017.

BBC Radio Wiltshire is a BBC Local Radio station serving Wiltshire, England. Launched in 1989 as *BBC Wiltshire Sound*, it was later split into *BBC Radio Swindon* and *BBC Radio Wiltshire* before reunifying in 2008. Broadcasting from Swindon, it provides local news, talk, and music on FM, DAB, Freeview, and BBC Sounds.

The Fairmont Century Plaza is a landmark 19-story luxury hotel in Los Angeles. Located in Century City, the hotel forms a sweeping crescent design fronting the Avenue of the Stars, adjacent to the twin Century Plaza Towers and the 2000 Avenue of the Stars complex. At the time of its opening in 1966, the Century Plaza Hotel was the highest building in Century City, with views extending all the way to the Pacific Ocean. It was also the first hotel to have color televisions in all of its rooms. The hotel closed for renovations in 2016, and reopened on September 27, 2021. It is now operated by Fairmont Hotels and Resorts.

The 1960 Louisiana gubernatorial election was held on April 19, 1960.

Primary elections were held in two rounds on December 5, 1959, and January 9, 1960. After defeating Chep Morrison in a Democratic primary which featured some of the most racially charged campaign rhetoric in Louisiana political history, Jimmie Davis was elected to his second nonconsecutive term as governor after defeating the Republican candidate, Francis Grevemberg, in the general election.
The history of the Jews in South Africa has been marked by periods of official and unofficial antisemitism.

Ghatak: Lethal is an Indian 1996 Hindi-language action thriller film directed by Rajkumar Santoshi, starring Sunny Deol, Meenakshi Seshadri, and Amrish Puri, while Danny Denzongpa plays the antagonist. Marking Santoshi's third collaboration with Deol, Seshadri, and Puri after Ghayal (1990) and Damini (1993), it earned ₹440 million worldwide to become the fourth highest-grossing Bollywood film of the year, and won three awards including Best Supporting Actor for Puri at the 42nd Filmfare Awards, where it was also nominated for three other awards: Best Director for Santoshi, Best Actor for Deol, and Best Villain for Denzongpa. Ghatak is Seshadri's last film to date. The film was later remade in Telugu as Aapthudu starring Rajasekhar and Anjala Zaveri.

Pierre Marinovitch (1 August 1898 – 2 October 1919) was a French-Serb fighter ace of World War I, credited with 21 aerial victories. Nicknamed "Marino," he flew with *Escadrille Spa.94* and became known as "The Youngest Ace" due to his age. Decorated with the *Légion d'honneur* and *Croix de Guerre*, he died in a post-war flying accident in Brussels.

Benton Township is a township in Adair County, Missouri, United States. It covers 80.3 square miles (207.9 km²) and includes the city of Kirksville, the county seat. As of 2010, its population was 19,700. The township contains Truman State University and major highways, including U.S. Route 63.

Allobates femoralis, commonly known as the brilliant-thighed poison frog, is a small, diurnal frog native to the Amazon basin and tropical eastern South America. It is known for its complex courtship behavior, including visual, acoustic, and tactile signals. Males exhibit territoriality and polygyny, while females show selective movement influenced by mating and environmental factors. The species is listed as Least Concern by the IUCN due to its stable population and wide distribution.
Cornelia Livia Orestina (her cognomen is sometimes given in the diminutive form Orestilla) was the second wife of the Roman Emperor Caligula in AD 37 or 38.

"Tight Connection to My Heart (Has Anybody Seen My Love)" is a song by Bob Dylan, released in 1985 as the lead single from his album *Empire Burlesque*. Originally recorded in 1983 as *"Someone's Got a Hold of My Heart"* during *Infidels* sessions, the final version features reworked lyrics with references to Humphrey Bogart films. The song blends rock and new wave elements, with backing vocals and overdubs added later. It reached the Top 40 in Belgium and New Zealand and has been praised as a standout track on an otherwise uneven album. A music video, directed by Paul Schrader, was filmed in Tokyo. The song has been covered by artists such as John Martyn and featured in the musical *Girl from the North Country*.

The 1957 Alberta liquor plebiscite was a provincial referendum held to decide whether to loosen liquor regulations in Alberta, which had remained restrictive since the Prohibition era. The vote passed by a wide margin, allowing for new liquor store locations, though rural areas largely opposed the change. A second question in Calgary, Edmonton, and surrounding areas asked whether "mixed drinking" (allowing men and women to drink together in bars) should be permitted—this measure passed overwhelmingly. The plebiscite was held alongside a vote on establishing an egg marketing board to reduce costs. The results led to the end of gender-segregated bars in major cities, with the rest of the province following in 1967.

Janet Elizabeth Haworth is a Welsh Conservative politician who was a Member of the Welsh Assembly (AM) for the North Wales Region between 2015 and 2016.

Haworth ran a guest house from 2005 with her partner, Dennis Oliver. She worked for Shell in Aberdeenshire until she retired to Llandudno in about 2003, and became a town and county councillor.

The Habbari dynasty (Arabic: حباري خاندان) was an Arab Muslim dynasty that ruled the Emirate of Mansura in Sindh from 854 to 1011 CE, nominally under the Abbasid Caliphate. Founded by ‘Umar ibn ‘Abd al-Aziz al-Habbari, the dynasty emerged after the weakening of Abbasid control, establishing Mansura as its capital. The Habbaris governed much of central and southern Sindh, including parts of Balochistan, while maintaining trade routes that enriched their realm. Though they pledged nominal allegiance to the Abbasids, they ruled independently. The dynasty fostered Sunni Islam, though Ismaili Shi'ism later gained influence. The Habbari emirate declined in the early 11th century and was eventually conquered by the Ghaznavids. Their rule marked a transitional period between Arab caliphal rule and later Indo-Islamic dynasties in Sindh.
Samuel Lord (1803-1889) was a British-born American retail millionaire who founded Lord & Taylor. It was the oldest luxury department store in the United States.

The Mercedes-Benz W140 is the third generation of the S-Class, produced from 1991 to 1998. It succeeded the W126 and was available as a sedan (in standard and long-wheelbase variants), a coupé (C140), and an extended Pullman limousine. Designed under Bruno Sacco, the W140 introduced advanced features such as double-pane windows, electronic stability control, and a V12 engine—Mercedes-Benz's first in a passenger car. Its development was delayed to compete with BMW's V12-powered E32 7 Series, leading to significant cost overruns. Despite its engineering excellence, the W140 faced criticism for its high price and size, especially amid competition from Japanese luxury brands. The model line included inline-six, V8, and V12 petrol engines, as well as turbocharged diesel options. The W140 was succeeded by the W220 in 1998.

Fish fingers (or fish sticks) are a processed seafood product made from whitefish, battered or breaded, and typically fried. Originating in the United Kingdom, they gained popularity after World War II. Commercial production began in 1953 in the U.S., while in the UK, Clarence Birdseye popularized cod-based versions. They are commonly served as a quick meal or snack.

A publicist is a person whose job is to generate and manage publicity for a company, a brand, or public figure – especially a celebrity – or for a work such as a book, film, or album. Publicists are public relations specialists who have the role to maintain and represent the images of individuals, rather than representing an entire corporation or business. Publicists are also hired by public figures who want to maintain or protect their image. Publicists brand their clients by getting magazine, TV, newspaper, and website coverage. Most top-level publicists work in private practice, handling multiple clients.

The term publicist was coined by the legal scholar Francis Lieber to describe the public-like role of internationalists during the late nineteenth century. Publicists are sometimes called flacks which traces back to Gene Flack, who was a well-known movie publicist in the 1930s.
Paulus Vallius (fl. 1580s–1622) was an Italian Jesuit philosopher and logician whose teachings influenced Galileo. He taught at the Collegio Romano and later published *Logica* (1622).

Man Mountain Dean (born Frank Simmons Leavitt; June 30, 1891 – May 29, 1953) was an American professional wrestler, actor, and U.S. Army veteran. Known for his imposing size and long beard, he wrestled in over 6,000 matches and appeared in films before retiring to Georgia.

The olfactory tract is a bundle of nerve fibers in the brain that transmits smell information from the olfactory bulb to the olfactory cortex. It divides into medial and lateral striae, ending in structures like the olfactory tubercle and amygdala. Damage to the tract can cause anosmia (loss of smell), as seen in Kallmann syndrome.

Ecological sanitation (ecosan) is a sustainable approach to sanitation that safely recycles nutrients from human excreta for agricultural use while minimizing reliance on non-renewable resources. Defined in 2012 by Swedish experts, ecosan systems prioritize resource recovery, pathogen reduction, and environmental protection. Unlike conventional sanitation, ecosan emphasizes closed-loop systems, often using urine-diverting dry toilets (UDDTs) or other technologies to separate and treat waste streams. The concept addresses global challenges such as phosphorus scarcity, food security, and water pollution. However, critics highlight concerns over cost, user acceptance, and safety. Ecosan has historical roots in traditional agricultural practices and gained modern recognition through initiatives by organizations like Sida and GIZ, as well as research on safe reuse methods.

Raquel Torres Cerdán, also Raquel Torres (born 19 November 1948) is a Mexican anthropologist and restauranteur, who has worked to record, preserve and showcase the cuisines of the indigenous peoples of Veracruz, through her restaurants and food writing.

Poot Mae Nam Khong (Thai: พูดแม่น้ำโขง) is a Thai supernatural drama television series that has aired in multiple versions (1992–1993, 2008, and 2022). The story follows Dr. Akkanee, who investigates paranormal activity in a Mekong riverside village, where he encounters a vengeful spirit tied to his past life. The series blends romance, horror, and folklore, becoming a ratings success, particularly in its 2008 run on BBTV Channel 7.

The Caister Murdoch System (CMS) was a major natural gas collection, processing and transportation system in the UK sector of the southern North Sea. It comprised 11 platforms, 8 subsea wellhead completions and interconnecting pipelines centered about 155 km east of Flamborough Head Yorkshire. It operated from 1993 to 2018.

The Philippine Center is an agency of the Philippine Government in New York City and San Francisco in the United States. The New York City structure houses the Philippine Mission to the United Nations, the Philippine Consulate General, and the overseas offices of the Department of Trade and Industry and the Department of Tourism. The Philippine Center Management (PCMB) manages the building and its properties. It is committed to "nurture, promote, and propagate Philippine culture, encourage foreign tourists to visit the Philippines, expand foreign markets of Philippine products, and enhance the image of the Philippines."

Qi Xin (born November 3, 1926) is a Chinese revolutionary and educator, best known as the widow of former Chinese Communist Party (CCP) leader Xi Zhongxun and the mother of current Chinese leader Xi Jinping. A veteran of the Second Sino-Japanese War, she studied at the Counter-Japanese Military and Political University and later worked in education and propaganda. She has been portrayed as a key influence on her family's political legacy.

Yvette Paula Fielding (born 23 September 1968) is an English television presenter, producer and actress. She became the youngest presenter on Blue Peter aged 18, and one of her episodes was subsequently voted the "Favourite Blue Peter moment" ever. With her husband Karl Beattie, she presented the Most Haunted series on the Living channel, via their own production company, followed by Ghosthunting With., establishing Fielding as 'first lady of the paranormal'. She has appeared in a wide range of other programmes, from The Wright Stuff to Through the Keyhole and I'm A Celebrity. Get Me Out of Here!.

Jagadishpur is a census town in Bally Jagachha CD Block of Howrah Sadar subdivision in Howrah district in the Indian state of West Bengal. It is a part of Kolkata Urban Agglomeration.

Jagadishpur is under the jurisdiction of Liluah Police Station of Howrah City Police.

Jitendra Malik (born October 11, 1960) is an Indian-American computer scientist known for his contributions to computer vision, including anisotropic diffusion, normalized cuts, and R-CNN. A professor at UC Berkeley, he has received numerous awards, including the Computer Pioneer Award (2019).

Yvette Fielding (born 23 September 1968) is an English television presenter, producer, and author, best known as the youngest presenter of *Blue Peter* (1987–1992) and for hosting paranormal shows such as *Most Haunted* and *Ghosthunting With...*. She co-founded Antix Productions with her husband, Karl Beattie, producing supernatural programming. Fielding has also appeared in reality TV, including *I'm a Celebrity... Get Me Out of Here!* (2015), and authored books on ghost hunting and children's fiction. She lives in Cheshire with her family.

Eclecticism is a conceptual approach that does not hold rigidly to a single paradigm or set of assumptions, but instead draws upon multiple theories, styles, or ideas to gain complementary insights into a subject, or applies different theories in particular cases. However, this is often without conventions or rules dictating how or which theories were combined.

It can sometimes seem inelegant or lacking in simplicity, and eclectics are sometimes criticized for lack of consistency in their thinking. It is, however, common in many fields of study. For example, most psychologists accept certain aspects of behaviorism, but do not attempt to use the theory to explain all aspects of human behavior.

Eclecticism in ethics, philosophy, politics and religion is also known as syncretism.

Elizabeth Parr-Johnston CM (born 1939) is an American-Canadian economist and academic administrator. She served as president of Mount Saint Vincent University (1991–1996) and the University of New Brunswick (1996–2002). Earlier, she worked in Canada's federal civil service and held executive roles at Inco and Shell Canada. Parr-Johnston played a key role in the extraction of American diplomats during the 1979 Iran hostage crisis. A Member of the Order of Canada, she was honored for her contributions to education. She holds degrees from Wellesley College and Yale University.

JD Eicher is an American singer-songwriter, producer, and novelist from Youngstown, Ohio, known for his alt-pop and indie music. He began his career with the band *JD Eicher & the Goodnights*, releasing a trilogy of albums (*The Shape of Things*, *Shifting*, and *Into Place*) before transitioning to a solo act. His 2016 album *The Middle Distance* and his 2020 release *Court Street* further established his introspective songwriting style. Eicher gained wider recognition after collaborating with author Nicholas Sparks on the soundtrack for *Two by Two*, leading to national exposure. In 2022, he released *Majesto Sessions* alongside his debut novel, *The Lights Along Majesto*. A frequent performer at festivals and charity events, Eicher has opened for major acts like Dave Matthews Band and Maroon 5.

A debug port is a chip-level diagnostic interface (akin to a computer port) included in an integrated circuit to aid design, fabrication, development, bootstrapping, configuration, debugging, and post-sale in-system programming. In general terms, a debug port is not necessary for end-use function and is often hidden or disabled in finished products.

When constituted in a high availability or safety critical system, a debug port may continue to operate as a system monitor for integrity validation in the finished product. In low-cost or high-volume electronics, debug ports are sometimes left enabled to avoid costs of design changes, and can be used by modders and hackers to gain control of the device and/or access to extra functionality.

Due to their high complexity, debug ports are nearly universal in microprocessors (CPUs) and microcontrollers (MCUs). These are discussed below.
Ruth Cameron is a singer, actress, and music producer.

Jemima Anne Morrell (7 March 1832 – 13 October 1909) was an English traveller and illustrator. Morrell was born into a middle class family in Selby, Yorkshire and was a member of the Junior United Alpine Club, a club with majority women members that organised annual holiday trips. Morrell was one of the tourists who in 1863 partook in the first ever guided tour of Switzerland, led and conducted by Thomas Cook, making her one of the first modern international tourists. Her account of the journey was published in 1963 under the title Miss Jemima's Swiss Journal: The First Conducted Tour of Switzerland.

Kogiidae is a family comprising at least two extant species of Cetacea, the pygmy (Kogia breviceps) and dwarf (K. sima) sperm whales. As their common names suggest, they somewhat resemble sperm whales, with squared heads and small lower jaws, but are much smaller in size, with much shorter skulls and more notable dorsal fins than sperm whales. Kogiids are also characterized by a "false gill slit" behind their eyes.

A number of genera are extinct.

The Western Tapes, 1983 is an EP by American country rock band Lone Justice, released in 2018 by Omnivore Recordings. Recorded in 1983 at the Record Plant and Mystic Sound studios in Los Angeles, the six-track EP features early demo versions of songs later re-recorded for their 1985 debut album. Produced by Marvin Etzioni and Ryan Hedgecock, the recordings capture the band's raw energy and blend of country, rock, and punk influences. Critics praised the EP for its authenticity and Maria McKee's powerful vocals, with some noting that these versions surpassed later studio takes. The release followed *The Vaught Tapes, 1983* (2014) and preceded *Live at the Palomino, 1983* (2019).

Diana Lee Inosanto (born in Torrance, California) is an American actress, martial artist, film director, and author. The daughter of Dan Inosanto and goddaughter of Bruce Lee, she is known for her work in *The Mandalorian* and her 2008 film *The Sensei*.

Kalwaria Zebrzydowska (IPA-pl) is a town in southern Poland with 4,429 inhabitants (2007 estimate). As of 1999, it is situated in Lesser Poland or Małopolska (in Polish). Previously, the town was administered within the Voivodeship of Bielsko-Biała (1975–1998).

Baaji (‹See RfD› transl. elder sister) is a 2019 Pakistani romantic drama film, directed by Saqib Malik and produced by his Production company Page 33 Films. The film features Meera as Shameera Osman Khalid Butt as Rohail Khan/ Khalid Osman, Amna Ilyas as Neha, Mohsin Abbas Haider as Ajji, Ali Kazmi as Rammy, Nayyar Ejaz, and Nisho in pivotal roles. It is a dramatic tale of a fading Lollywood actress Shameera (Meera), who is not accepting that now her position in the industry is not much valuable. The story revolves around the relationship of Shameera with a Salon girl Neha (Amna Ilyas) who later becomes an assistant of Shameera and make this opportunity happens of working with an American Based Pakistani Director Rohail Khan (Osman Khalid Butt). Neha who belongs to a conservative lower middle class family then also make herself involved in Shameera's comeback movie titled Waapsi by helping them in rehearsals and script making so far she get a chance to take a supporting role in the movie. The film includes Ali Kazmi as Raamy (Shameera's Boyfriend) who is highly aggressive about Shameera. Mohsin Abbas Haider plays the love interest of neha's character, while the Legendary actor Nayyer Ejaz supports the film by his great comics and unforgettable role as Chand Kamal, film also includes numerous well-known faces from the industry as in cameo appearances.

SLINEX is a bilateral naval exercise between India and Sri Lanka, conducted periodically since at least 2013. The drills include anti-submarine warfare, surface firing, and maritime security operations, typically held off the coasts of Goa or Trincomalee.

Jack Ross (born in Auckland, New Zealand) is a writer, editor, and academic known for his experimental literature. A lecturer at Massey University, he has edited several literary magazines, including *brief* and *Landfall*. His work has been both praised and criticized within New Zealand avant-garde circles.
*Freerunner* (2011) is an American action thriller about eight parkour athletes forced into a deadly race with explosive collars. Directed by Lawrence Silverstein, it stars Sean Faris and Danny Dyer.

John Landsteiner (born May 19, 1990) is an American curler and two-time Olympian from Duluth, Minnesota. He competed in the 2014 Winter Olympics and won gold as part of John Shuster's team in the 2018 Winter Olympics.
