Shaheed Lateef (11 June 1913 – 16 April 1967) was Hindi film director, writer, and producer. He was the maker of films like Ziddi (1948) which launched Dev Anand's career and Arzoo (1950) starring Dilip Kumar and Kamini Kaushal.

Saint Melchior, or Melichior, was purportedly one of the Biblical Magi along with Caspar and Balthazar who visited the infant Jesus after he was born. Melchior was often referred to as the oldest member of the Magi. He was traditionally called the King of Persia and brought the gift of gold to Jesus. In the Western Christian church, he is regarded as a saint (as are the other two Magi).

Taptu was a British social media company founded in 2007, based in Cambridge. It initially launched a mobile search engine, optimized for phones and available via web and apps, before shutting it down in 2011. Its main product, My Taptu, was a social news aggregator for iPhone and Android, designed to combat "information overload" by organizing content into personalized streams. The company aimed to provide a single app for users' interests, differentiating itself through customization.

Academic ranks in the United States follow a hierarchy, typically including Professor, Associate Professor, and Assistant Professor for tenure-track faculty, with additional roles such as Lecturer, Instructor, and Adjunct Professor for non-tenure-track positions. Specialized ranks include Clinical Professor, Professor of Practice, and Distinguished Professor.

The State Seal of Japan (大日本國璽) is the official seal used for state documents, adopted in 1874. Made of gold, it bears the inscription "Seal of Great Japan" in seal script and is used primarily for imperial orders.
North Bengal State Library is the largest library in the North Bengal region of India. It is situated in the Cooch Behar city area.

"You're a Big Girl Now" is a song by Bob Dylan, released on his 15th studio album, Blood on the Tracks, in 1975. It is one of five songs on the album that Dylan initially recorded in New York City in September 1974 and then re-recorded in Minneapolis in December that year. The latter recording, made on December 27, 1974, became the album track.

The European Heritage Label is a recognition awarded by the European Union to buildings, documents, museums, archives, monuments or events which are seen as milestones in the creation of today's Europe. The program is managed by the European Commission.

Henry Hastings, 3rd Earl of Huntingdon, KG, KB (c. 1535 – 14 December 1595) was an English Puritan nobleman. Educated alongside the future Edward VI, he was briefly imprisoned by Mary I, and later considered by some as a potential successor to Elizabeth I. He hotly opposed the scheme to marry Mary, Queen of Scots, to the Duke of Norfolk, and was entrusted by Elizabeth to see that the Scottish queen did not escape at the time of the threatened uprising in 1569. He served as President of the Council of the North from 1572 until his death in 1595.

The Czech National Bank, (Lang-cs, ČNB) is the central bank and financial market supervisor in the Czech Republic, headquartered in Prague. It is and a member of the European System of Central Banks. It was established on 1 January 1993 from the division of the State Bank of Czechoslovakia as part of the process of dissolution of Czechoslovakia, together with the National Bank of Slovakia.

In accordance with its primary objective, the CNB sets monetary policy, issues banknotes and coins and manages the circulation of the Czech koruna, the payment system and settlement between banks. It also performs supervision of the banking sector, the capital market, the insurance industry, pension funds, credit unions and electronic money institutions, as well as foreign exchange supervision.

The Commissioner of Police, Delhi or Delhi Police Commissioner is the head of the Delhi Police, the Law enforcement agency of the 15 police districts (as of January 2019) of National Capital of India, Delhi.

Mouse-Warming is a 1952 Warner Bros. animated short directed by Chuck Jones. It follows a lovestruck mouse who repeatedly outwits Claude Cat while trying to woo a female neighbor. The cartoon features Jones' signature humor and visual gags.

Otto Kirchheimer (1905–1965) was a German-American jurist and political scientist known for his work on constitutional law, political justice, and party systems. A student of Carl Schmitt, he analyzed the collapse of the Weimar Republic and later critiqued Nazi Germany while in exile. His concept of the "catch-all party" remains influential in political science. After fleeing Nazi persecution, he worked for the U.S. Office of Strategic Services and taught at Columbia University and the New School for Social Research.

Tim Peters is a German political scientist, lawyer, and politician. Born in Hamburg, he studied law at TU Dresden, Paris Nanterre University, and Humboldt University of Berlin, later earning a PhD in political science with a dissertation on the anti-fascist tradition of the Party of Democratic Socialism. He worked in legal and policy advisory roles, including for the CDU/CSU parliamentary group, General Electric, and German business associations in Brussels. Since the mid-2000s, he has been based in Brussels, where he now works for the European Parliament. Politically active from a young age, Peters chaired the Junge Union in Berlin, ran for the CDU in the 2005 federal election, and later led the CDU's Brussels branch.

Wild Jack is a 1974 science fiction novel by John Christopher, set in a post-apocalyptic 23rd-century England. The story follows Clive Anderson, a privileged boy from the walled city of London, who is falsely accused of treason and sent to a brutal prison island. After escaping with two friends, he encounters the legendary outlaw Wild Jack and his band of rebels in the savage Outlands. Clive must choose between returning to the oppressive city or embracing true freedom in the wilderness. The novel explores themes of societal control, rebellion, and personal liberty in a fractured world.

Woo In-hee (Korean: 우인희; died 1981) was a North Korean actress and the mistress of Kim Jong-il. Known as "the most beautiful woman in North Korea," she starred in films such as *The Tale of Chunhyang* and earned the title of *People's Actress*. After an extramarital affair with Kim Jong-il, she was executed by firing squad in 1981 for revealing their relationship. Her name and image were later purged from North Korean media. A South Korean TV drama, *Until the Azalea Blooms*, depicts her life.

Monophysitism (/məˈnɒfɪsaɪˌtɪzəm/ or /məˈnɒfɪsɪˌtɪzəm/) or monophysism (/məˈnɒfɪzɪzəm/) is a Christological term derived from the Greek μόνος (‹See RfD› monos, "alone, solitary") and φύσις (‹See RfD› physis, a word that has many meanings but in this context means "nature"). It is defined as "a doctrine that in the person of the incarnated Word (that is, in Jesus Christ) there was only one nature—the divine".

Mariano Arcioni (born 2 April 1970) is an Argentine lawyer and politician who has served as Governor of Chubut since 2017. A member of *We Are All Chubut*, he was first elected as vice governor in 2015 before succeeding Mario Das Neves upon his death. His administration has faced protests over wage disputes and mining policies.

Joseph Heller (born April 10, 1941) is an Israeli zoologist and malacologist specializing in the taxonomy, biogeography, and reproductive biology of gastropods. His research focuses on the natural history of terrestrial and aquatic mollusks, including their adaptations to heat and desiccation, and he has developed biogeographic models for studying faunal responses to climate change. Since 1979, he has served as the academic curator of the National Molluscs Collection at the Hebrew University of Jerusalem. Heller has held leadership roles in scientific organizations, including as head of the Department of Zoology at Hebrew University and editor-in-chief of *The Israel Journal of Zoology*. In 2010, he was named an Honorary Member of the Zoological Society of Israel for his contributions to zoological research and education.

David Production (株式会社デイヴィッドプロダクション) is a Japanese animation studio founded in September 2007 by former Gonzo producers. Known for works like *JoJo's Bizarre Adventure*, it was acquired by Fuji TV in 2014. The studio's name references the biblical David and Goliath.

Gavan McCormack is a researcher specializing in East Asia who is Emeritus Professor and Visiting Fellow, Division of Pacific and Asian History of the Australian National University. He is also a coordinator of an award-winning open access journal The Asia-Pacific Journal: Japan Focus.

Clare Rita Mary Maguire (born 15 September 1988) is an English singer-songwriter. She was signed to Universal Music Group, Polydor Records in 2009. She was rated in fifth place in the BBC Sound of 2011 list of the top 15 most promising new artists. Maguire was also singled out as one of MTV's Brand New: For 2011 Acts. Her voice has been compared to Stevie Nicks and Annie Lennox. Maguire's debut album, Light After Dark, was released on 25 February 2011 peaking at number seven in the Official UK Top 40, and achieving the BPI Silver Sales Award in the UK.

In 2016, she announced the forthcoming release of her second album Stranger Things Have Happened on 27 May with the song "Elizabeth Taylor". The album was released on Virgin Records.

The architecture of the Deccan sultanates reflects the distinctive blend of Persian, Indian, and regional styles developed under the Bahmani and its successor sultanates—Ahmadnagar, Bidar, Bijapur, Golkonda, and Gulbarga. Notable structures include Ahmadnagar's palaces (Farah Bakhsh Bagh, Hasht Bihisht Bagh) and tombs; Bidar's grand fort, Mahmud Gawan Madrasa, and Bahmani tombs; and Bijapur's monumental Gol Gumbaz (with one of the world's largest pre-modern domes) and Ibrahim Rouza. Golkonda is renowned for its impregnable fort, Qutb Shahi tombs, and iconic Charminar, while Gulbarga features early Bahmani monuments like the Great Mosque and Haft Gumbad complex. These sultanates left a lasting architectural legacy, characterized by massive domes, intricate stucco work, and innovative urban planning.

Kurihara (栗原市) is a city in Miyagi Prefecture, Japan, known for its agricultural landscape, hot springs, and scenic beauty around Mount Kurikoma. Established in 2005 through the merger of several towns and villages, it covers 804.97 km² and had a population of 66,565 as of 2020. The economy is primarily based on rice farming, with historical ties to mining. The city features attractions like Lake Izunuma's lotus flowers and seasonal foliage.

The Czech National Bank (CNB) is the central bank of the Czech Republic, headquartered in Prague. Established in 1993 following the dissolution of Czechoslovakia, it succeeded the State Bank of Czechoslovakia. The CNB's primary mandate is ensuring price stability, targeting an inflation rate of around 2%, while also supporting sustainable economic growth. It regulates the Czech financial sector, including banking, insurance, and capital markets, and maintains exclusive authority over the issuance of the Czech koruna (CZK). Although the Czech Republic is an EU member, it has indefinitely postponed adopting the euro. The bank is governed by a seven-member Bank Board, led by a governor appointed by the president. Its monetary policy tools include open market operations, interest rate adjustments, and foreign exchange interventions.

Birna Arnbjörnsdóttir (born 1952) is an Icelandic linguist. She is Professor of Second Language Studies at the University of Iceland, Director of the Vigdís Finnbogadóttir Institute of Foreign Languages and Dean of the Faculty of Languages and Cultures.

The Western Tapes, 1983 is an EP by American country rock band Lone Justice, released on 23 November 2018 by Omnivore Recordings. It consists of demo recordings made in 1983 prior to their self-titled debut release in 1985 and was recorded by the original Lone Justice lineup consisting of Maria McKee, Ryan Hedgecock, Dave Harrington and Don Willens. All tracks are previously unissued, except "Drugstore Cowboy" which was included on the compilation album This World Is Not My Home in 1999. David Mansfield, who has toured with Bob Dylan and later became a member of Bruce Hornsby and the Range, guests on pedal steel guitar and fiddle.

Canajoharie Creek is a 69.22-square-mile (179.3 km²) tributary of the Mohawk River in central New York, flowing through Otsego and Montgomery counties. Originating near Cherry Valley, it travels northeast, passing through the Canajoharie Gorge and over the 45-foot (14 m) Canajoharie Falls before joining the Mohawk River at the village of Canajoharie. The creek's name derives from the Mohawk term for "the pot that washes itself," referencing a deep pothole known as the "boiling pot." Historically significant, the area was settled by Europeans in the 18th century and used by General James Clinton's army during the American Revolution. The creek supports brown trout and other fish species, with water quality rated Class C by the DEC.

In various science/engineering applications, such as independent component analysis, image analysis, genetic analysis, speech recognition, manifold learning, and time delay estimation it is useful to estimate the differential entropy of a system or process, given some observations.

The simplest and most common approach uses histogram-based estimation, but other approaches have been developed and used, each with its own benefits and drawbacks. The main factor in choosing a method is often a trade-off between the bias and the variance of the estimate, although the nature of the (suspected) distribution of the data may also be a factor.

Trochilinae is one of the six subfamilies that make up the hummingbird family Trochilidae.

The subfamily is divided into three tribes: Lampornithini (mountain gems) containing 18 species, Mellisugini (bees) containing 37 species and Trochilini (emeralds) containing 115 species.

Natalia Maliszewska (born 16 September 1995) is a Polish short track speed skater. She is a 500m World Champion (2018), European Champion (2019), and World Cup winner (2018–19). She has competed in two Winter Olympics (2018, 2022).
Li Shuai (born 18 August 1982) is a Chinese former professional footballer who played as a goalkeeper for Qingdao Jonoon, Guangzhou Evergrande, and Shanghai Shenhua.

Alice – A Fight For Life is a documentary featuring 47-year-old Alice Jefferson, a British woman who developed malignant pleural mesothelioma thirty years after working for nine months at Cape Insulation's Acre Mill asbestos plant in Hebden Bridge, West Yorkshire. The film also explored the health issues surrounding the manufacture and use of asbestos products. Described by The Guardian newspaper as "a momentous film", the programme also explicitly linked asbestos with cancer, and attacked what it perceived as the government's complacency in limiting the manufacture and use of asbestos in Britain.

Alice died of mesothelioma in February 1982, a month after filming for the programme had ended. She left two children: Paul and Patsy aged, 15 and 5 respectively.

Kogiidae is a family of toothed whales comprising the pygmy (*Kogia breviceps*) and dwarf (*Kogia sima*) sperm whales. These small cetaceans inhabit tropical and temperate oceans worldwide, avoiding polar regions. They are deep-diving suction feeders, primarily consuming squid. Kogiids possess unique adaptations, including a spermaceti organ, an "ink sac" for predator defense, and highly asymmetric skulls. Genetic and anatomical evidence suggests a close relationship with sperm whales (*Physeteridae*), though their taxonomy remains debated. Both species face threats from fisheries and plastic pollution.
Antisemitism in South Africa has manifested in various forms, from Nazi-aligned policies before apartheid to far-right movements and modern political rhetoric.
Kleiner Tiergarten ("Small Tiergarten") is a park in Moabit, Berlin, Germany.

Cardea or Carda was the ancient Roman goddess of the hinge (Latin cardo, cardinis), Roman doors being hung on pivot hinges. The Augustan poet Ovid conflates her with another archaic goddess named Carna, whose festival was celebrated on the Kalends of June and for whom he gives the alternative name Cranê or Cranea, a nymph. Ovid's conflation of the goddesses is likely to have been his poetic invention, but it has also been conjectured that Carna was a contracted form of Cardina, and at minimum Ovid was observing that their traditions were congruent.

Rosa Amelia Guzmán (fl. 1935–1967) was a Salvadoran feminist activist and politician. A co-founder of *Tribuna Femenina* and the Liga Femenina Salvadoreña, she campaigned for women's suffrage and legal equality, achieved in 1950. In 1956, she became one of El Salvador's first female legislators. She was married to former president Arturo Araujo and later received a state pension after his death.
Maureen Sullivan is an American librarian, educator, and organizational consultant who served as the president of the American Library Association from 2012 to 2013.
Pierre Martin Victor Richard de Laprade (13 January 1812 – 13 December 1883), known as Victor de Laprade, was a French poet and critic.

L. David Mech (born January 18, 1937) is an American biologist and ecologist renowned for his research on wolf behavior and ecology. He earned his B.S. from Cornell University (1958) and Ph.D. from Purdue University (1962), where he studied wolves on Isle Royale, leading to his first book, *The Wolves of Isle Royale* (1966). Mech later conducted long-term research in Minnesota’s Superior National Forest and on Ellesmere Island, where he observed wolves with minimal fear of humans, providing unique insights into pack dynamics.  

A senior research scientist with the U.S. Geological Survey and adjunct professor at the University of Minnesota, Mech has authored or co-authored over 380 scientific papers and 11 books, including *The Wolf: The Ecology and Behavior of an Endangered Species* (1970) and *Wolves: Behavior, Ecology, and Conservation* (2003). His work has influenced wolf conservation and management policies. An advocate for regulated hunting and trapping, Mech supports state-managed wolf populations where they are no longer endangered. He remains an active researcher and writer on wolf ecology.
Jean Garrabé (1931–2020) was a French psychiatrist and historian of psychiatry.

Fish fingers (British English) or fish sticks (American English) are a processed food made using a whitefish, such as cod, hake, haddock, shark or pollock, which has been battered or breaded. They are commonly available in the frozen food section of supermarkets. They can be baked in an oven, grilled, shallow fried, or deep-fried.
Birdmonster is an American indie rock band formed in San Francisco in 2004.

KushCo Holdings is an American company that sells packaging, containers, and other ancillary products for the cannabis industry. It is based in Garden Grove, California and is publicly traded on the New York OTCQB marketplace under the stock ticker symbol, KSHB.

FC Emmen is a Dutch professional football club based in Emmen, founded in 1925. After decades as an amateur club, it turned professional in 1985 and adopted its current name in 2005. The team plays at De Oude Meerdijk (capacity: 8,600) and competes in the Eerste Divisie. Emmen earned its first promotion to the Eredivisie in 2018, spending three seasons in the top flight before relegation in 2021. The club has also participated in multiple Eerste Divisie playoffs and hosted matches during the 2005 FIFA World Youth Championship.

The Technological Educational Institute of Peloponnese (TEI of Peloponnese) was a public university system in Greece, active from 1989 to 2019. Headquartered in Kalamata, it comprised four schools with six departments, including engineering and health sciences. It was abolished in 2019 and partially merged into the University of Peloponnese.

Bud Wildman (born June 3, 1946) is a Canadian former politician who served as an Ontario MPP for Algoma from 1975 to 1999. A member of the Ontario New Democratic Party (NDP), he held cabinet positions as Minister of Natural Resources, Minister of the Environment and Energy, and Minister responsible for Native Affairs under Premier Bob Rae. He briefly served as interim NDP leader in 1996. After retiring from provincial politics, he ran unsuccessfully for the federal NDP in Sault Ste. Marie in 2000. Wildman later worked as a consultant and served on various boards, including Algoma University.

Kalwaria Zebrzydowska is a town in southern Poland, known for its 17th-century monastery and pilgrimage park, a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Founded in 1617, it is a major religious center and was frequently visited by Pope John Paul II.

Hugues Alexandre Joseph Meunier (1768–1831) was a French military officer who served in the Revolutionary and Napoleonic Wars, rising to brigadier general and later lieutenant general. He commanded key campaigns, including in the Vendée and Ireland, and held administrative roles under Napoleon.

The Waldorf-Astoria Hotel and Residence Tower is a proposed 107-story mixed-use skyscraper in Chicago, Illinois. Designed by DeStefano & Partners and developed by The Prime Group, the tower would rise to 1,265 feet (386 m), making it one of the tallest buildings in the city. Plans include a 325-room Waldorf-Astoria hotel (managed by Hilton) and 300 luxury condominiums, with six duplex penthouses on the upper floors. The design features a tapered, slender profile to minimize blocked views, adhering to Chicago’s architectural trends. Construction was initially expected to begin in 2009 but is now projected for 2026, with completion by 2031. If built, it would be part of Hilton’s expansion of the Waldorf-Astoria brand beyond its flagship New York location.
John Barnard (1705–1784) was an 18th century English shipbuilder serving the Royal Navy.

An ultrasonic motor is a type of piezoelectric motor powered by the ultrasonic vibration of a component, the stator, placed against another component, the rotor or slider depending on the scheme of operation (rotation or linear translation). Ultrasonic motors differ from other piezoelectric motors in several ways, though both typically use some form of piezoelectric material, most often lead zirconate titanate and occasionally lithium niobate or other single-crystal materials. The most obvious difference is the use of resonance to amplify the vibration of the stator in contact with the rotor in ultrasonic motors. Ultrasonic motors also offer arbitrarily large rotation or sliding distances, while piezoelectric actuators are limited by the static strain that may be induced in the piezoelectric element.

One common application of ultrasonic motors is in camera lenses where they are used to move lens elements as part of the auto-focus system. Ultrasonic motors replace the noisier and often slower micro-motor in this application.
Sprung is an American crime comedy television series created by Greg Garcia. It premiered on the streaming service Amazon Freevee on August 19, 2022.

The Manned Spaceflight Engineer Program was an effort by the United States Air Force to train American military personnel as payload specialists for United States Department of Defense missions on the Space Shuttle program.

In object-oriented programming, method cascading is syntax which allows multiple methods to be called on the same object. This is particularly applied in fluent interfaces.

For example, in Dart, the cascade:

is equivalent to the individual calls:

Method cascading is much less common than method chaining – it is found only in a handful of object-oriented languages, while chaining is very common. A form of cascading can be implemented using chaining, but this restricts the interface; see comparison with method chaining, below.

Great Wishford is a village and civil parish in Wiltshire, England, about 4 miles northwest of Salisbury. With a population of 368 (2011), it is known for its historic Oak Apple Day celebrations, where villagers claim ancient wood-gathering rights in Grovely Wood. The Church of St Giles, largely rebuilt in the 19th century, is Grade II* listed. The village has a primary school and a pub, the Royal Oak.

Yaser Abdel Said (born January 27, 1957) is an Egyptian-American convicted murderer who was added to the FBI's Ten Most Wanted Fugitives list in 2014 for the 2008 killings of his two teenage daughters, Amina and Sarah Said, in Irving, Texas. A former taxi driver, Said had a history of domestic abuse and controlling behavior toward his family. After his daughters fled with their mother to escape his abuse, he lured them back and shot them in his taxi. Sarah managed to call 911 before dying, identifying her father as the shooter.  

Said evaded capture for 12 years before being arrested in August 2020 in Justin, Texas, with the help of his son, Islam Said, and brother, Yassein Said, who were later convicted of aiding him. In 2022, Yaser Said was found guilty of capital murder and sentenced to life in prison without parole. The case gained national attention and was featured in the documentary *The Price of Honor* (2014) and an episode of *The Hunt with John Walsh* (2015).

Rosa Amelia Guzmán was a Salvadoran journalist, feminist and suffragette. Her 1950 speech to the Constituent Assembly was instrumental in women gaining, not just the right to vote, but the rights of citizenship on 14 September. She was one of the first four women elected to serve in the Legislative Assembly of El Salvador.
Southfield Football Club, occasionally referred to as Birmingham Southfield, was an English football club based in Birmingham, then in Warwickshire.

Cabot Tower is a tower in St. John's, Newfoundland and Labrador, situated on Signal Hill. Construction of the tower began in 1898 to commemorate the 400th anniversary of John Cabot's discovery of Newfoundland, and Queen Victoria's Diamond Jubilee.

In 1901, Guglielmo Marconi received the first trans-Atlantic wireless message at a position near the tower, the letter "S" in Morse Code sent from Poldhu, Cornwall, United Kingdom. Cabot Tower is now the centre of the Signal Hill National Historic Site of Canada, with walking trails and an interpretation centre.

Thrilling is an online marketplace for vintage clothing, apparel and home goods. The company partners with independent vintage and secondhand clothing stores that use the platform to list, sell, and ship inventory. Thrilling was founded by Shilla Kim-Parker and Brad Mallow in 2018, In 2021, the company received $8.47 million in series A funding.

Thrilling has partnered with Banana Republic, Academy Award nominated costume designer Ruth E. Carter, and offers a studio service that uses the company's network of stores to find authentic period clothing for film and television productions. In 2020, Thrilling partnered with stylist Zerina Akers of "Black Owned Everything" to curate an online collection of vintage items from black-owned stores.

As of January 2022, Thrilling hosted listings from more than 900 vintage stores in approximately 200 cities and planned to use series A funding to improve technology such as partner store analytics, partially automating the item listing process, and adding personalization for customers shopping on the site.

In May 2022, Thrilling introduced a home goods section that allows thrift shop owners to list and sell vintage home decor items.

Elizabeth Parr-Johnston, CM (born Elizabeth H. Parr in 1939 in New York, NY) is a Canadian business woman. She is the Managing Partner of Parr-Johnston Consultants, an economic policy consultancy based in Chester Basin, Nova Scotia. Parr-Johnston is a past president of two Canadian Universities, a recipient of the 125th Anniversary of the Confederation of Canada Medal in 1992, the Queen Elizabeth II Golden Jubilee Medal in 2002 and the Order of Canada in 2008.

Raised in Essex Fells, New Jersey, she attended Grover Cleveland High School (since renamed as James Caldwell High School).

Red God (1954–?) was an American-bred Thoroughbred racehorse who won the Richmond Stakes (1956) and Roseben Handicap (1958). Later a stallion in Ireland, he sired 10 stakes winners. Bred and owned by Harry F. Guggenheim, he was trained in England by Cecil Boyd-Rochfort.

Peterson v Minister of Safety & Security (2009) was a South African Supreme Court of Appeal case concerning police liability for injuries caused during a crowd confrontation. The court ruled that officers' use of force was justified by necessity, dismissing the plaintiff's claim for damages. The decision clarified the legal requirements for the defence of necessity in delictual claims.
Arena (stylized as FOX Arena) is an Australian pay television channel owned by Foxtel, featuring entertainment and lifestyle programming.
Norway 1985 is a 1985 wargame by Strategic Simulations, simulating NATO-Soviet conflict in Norway. Players control either side in single-player or two-player mode on Apple II and Commodore 64.

The Caister Murdoch System (CMS) was a group of offshore gas fields in the UK North Sea, developed by Conoco and Total. Discovered between 1968 and 1986, production began in 1993 via the Murdoch platform, with gas exported to the Theddlethorpe terminal. The system included multiple satellite fields and compression infrastructure before ceasing operations in 2018.

Slaughter Studios is a 2002 comedy/horror film. It was directed by Brian Katkin, with the screenplay by John Huckert and Dan Acre, from a story by Huckert, Acre, and Damian Akhavi. The film, 86 minutes long and rated R, was released through New Concorde.

It was the last film shot at Roger Corman's studios in Venice Beach, California, and it only had a 12-day shooting schedule. The studio was being torn down during the production.

The film began life as a remake of Slumber Party Massacre, and writers John Huckert and Dan Acre were hired to pen the script. But after visiting the soon-to-be-demolished studio they came up with this idea instead. Producer Damien Akhavi helped them write the story. Footage from the original Slumber Party Massacre can still be seen during a murder sequence as an in-joke for the production crew.

Though principal photography took place in February 2001, the final sequences were not filmed until July 2002. The long break was necessitated by the fact that the scenes were to be shot in Malibu, but the rainfall was unusually heavy, so the completion of the film was postponed. Director Katkin shot another film during the break in this movie's production.

Gemeinhardt Co. is a manufacturer of flutes and piccolos. These musical instruments are developed by this company for all levels of musicians, beginners to professionals. It is owned by its major supplier, Angel Industries Co. Ltd of Taiwan, widely acknowledged as the premier manufacturer of woodwind musical instruments. The Gemeinhardt Company is headquartered in Elkhart, Indiana, where many of its instruments are still made.

Rainbow War is an animated allegorical tale about three isolated kingdoms—Blue, Red, and Golden—each enforcing a single color. Conflict erupts when their armies clash, mixing paints and discovering new colors. After nearly losing the Red Princess in battle, they unite, embracing diversity and creating the first rainbow, symbolizing harmony. The war ends with interconnected, colorful kingdoms.
Maria Clara Eimmart (27 May 1676 – 29 October 1707), was a German astronomer, engraver and designer. She was the daughter and assistant of Georg Christoph Eimmart the Younger.

Titus Haterius Nepos was an eques who held a number of imperial Roman positions during the reigns of Trajan and Hadrian. The two most important posts were praefectus vigilum or commander of the vigiles or nightwatch, and praefectus or governor of Egypt (120-124). According to Fergus Millar, Nepos was the first eques who was promoted from regular procuratorial posts into a proper secretarial appointment.

Vena, currently DNA Latin Group, is an American bachata group formed by members Lenny Santos and Max Santos from Aventura alongside ex-vocalist Steven Tejada from Xtreme. They are known for their hits such as "Sangre de Mis Venas", "Corazon de Hierro (featuring Teodoro Reyes & Frank Reyes)", "Señora", "Dile a El", "Por Mentiras", among others. VENA alongside Larry Hernandez were nominated for a Billboard Premious Musica Mexicana award in 2012.
Arthur is a town in Chippewa County in the U.S. state of Wisconsin. The population was 759 at the 2010 census. The unincorporated communities of Cobban, Crescent, and Drywood are located in the town.

Building officials of developed countries are generally the jurisdictional administrator of building and construction codes, engineering calculation supervision, permits, facilities management, and accepted construction procedures.

An extraocular implant is a form of body modification involving jewelry placed within the eye. Developed in the Netherlands in 2002, the procedure is performed by ophthalmologists and uses small platinum alloy pieces.

Great Wishford is a village and civil parish in the Wylye Valley in Wiltshire, England, about three miles (5 km) north of Wilton and five miles (8 km) northwest of Salisbury. The village lies west of a bend in the River Wylye and has a triangular street layout comprising South Street, West Street and Station Road.

Ruth Ella Moore (May 19, 1903 in Columbus, Ohio – July 19, 1994) was an American bacteriologist and microbiologist, who, in 1933, became the first African-American woman to be awarded a Ph.D. in a natural science. She was a professor of bacteriology at Howard University. A decade later, she was installed as the head of the department of bacteriology, which she renamed to the department of microbiology. During that period she was promoted to associate professor of microbiology.

She published her research on tuberculosis, immunology, dental caries, the response of gut microorganisms to antibiotics, and the blood types of African-Americans.

Although there are gaps in the official personnel records of the university, Moore is believed to have continued to teach and conduct her research at Howard until 1973, after which she held the position of associate professor emeritus of microbiology until 1990.

Moore also gained recognition as a fashion designer.

Great Lakes Depression is a major freshwater basin and wetland system in Mongolia, spanning approximately 100,000 km² (39,000 sq mi). Located in a desert steppe region, it consists of interconnected shallow lakes with extensive reed belts, serving as a critical habitat for migratory birds such as the Eurasian spoonbill, black stork, and white-tailed eagle. The wetlands also support rare fish species, including endemic Altai osmans (*Oreoleuciscus*), graylings (*Thymallus*), and stone loaches (*Triplophysa*). Despite its arid surroundings, the depression remains an ecologically significant area in Central Asia, though some species, like the great white pelican, are now scarce in the region.

Gilad Efrat (born 1969) is an Israeli painter known for his layered, abstract landscapes and portraits. A professor at Shenkar College, his work explores memory, geopolitics, and the interplay between photography and painting.

Following the termination of hostilities in World War II, the Allies were in control of the defeated Axis countries. Anticipating the defeat of Germany and Japan, they had already set up the European Advisory Commission and a proposed Far Eastern Advisory Commission to make recommendations for the post war period. Accordingly, they managed their control of the defeated countries through Allied Commissions, often referred to as Allied Control Commissions (ACC), consisting of representatives of the major Allies.
The Great Seal of Japan (国璽, kokuji) is one of the national seals of Japan and is used as the official seal of state.

Grand Loop Road is a historic scenic roadway in Yellowstone National Park, Wyoming, forming a 142-mile (229 km) loop that connects the park's major geothermal features, lakes, and canyons. Constructed by the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers and later maintained by the National Park Service, it was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 2003. The road begins near Mammoth Hot Springs, passing through Golden Gate Canyon, Norris Geyser Basin, and the Firehole River Valley, where it links to the Old Faithful area. It then crosses the Continental Divide at Craig Pass, follows Yellowstone Lake, and traverses the Grand Canyon of the Yellowstone before looping back via Dunraven Pass and Tower Fall. Major junctions include access roads to West Yellowstone, Grand Teton National Park, and Cooke City, Montana. The route provides access to key park landmarks, including geyser basins, waterfalls, and historic lodges.
Dar Hammuda Pasha is an old palace in the medina of Tunis. It is considered one of the oldest and biggest palaces of the medina that kept their original architecture.

Royalty Exchange is an online marketplace founded in 2011 that allows investors to buy and sell music royalties. Headquartered in Denver, Colorado, it was acquired in 2015 by private investors, including CEO Matthew Smith. The company facilitates auctions and private syndicates for royalty streams, including catalogs from artists like Eminem, Cage the Elephant, and Dire Straits. In 2017, it launched Royalty Flow, a venture aimed at securitizing royalties for public investment, though its planned IPO was later canceled. Royalty Exchange also partners with digital distributors like Vydia to provide funding advances for artists. It has raised $6.4 million in funding and participated in the Techstars Music Accelerator.

Buttlejorrk is a parish of the County of Bourke located to the west of Sunbury, in Victoria, Australia and a neighbourhood within the locality of Diggers Rest. It was named in 1839 by the surveyor William Darke. A township developed and was initially known as Aitken's Gap. By the 1890s it had commonly become known as Buttlejorrk. A Post Office opened on 5 September 1856 as The Gap, was renamed Buttlejork (sic) in 1875 and closed in 1919. One of the early settlers in the area was George Millett. George and his wife Suzanne established the Bald Hill Hotel, the first staging stop out of Melbourne on the road to the goldfields at Bendigo. The hotel was licensed in 1853 until 1907. The Aitken's Gap Gaol, now located in the forecourt of the Sunbury Police Station stood beside the hotel

Hopatcong State Park is a 163-acre (0.66 km²) public recreation area located in Roxbury Township, New Jersey, on the shores of Lake Hopatcong. Established in 1922, the park offers swimming, fishing, boating, and winter activities like ice fishing and sledding. It features picnic areas, playgrounds, sports courts, and a boat ramp. The park also preserves historical elements, including the Lake Hopatcong Historical Museum, housed in a former Morris Canal lock tender’s house, and a display of canal technology, including a Scotch Turbine from the Morris Canal. Operated by the New Jersey Division of Parks and Forestry, it is a popular destination for outdoor recreation.

Sir Peter Edward Bairsto (3 August 1926 – 24 October 2017) was a senior Royal Air Force officer who served as Deputy Commander of Strike Command (1981–84). A decorated veteran, he was awarded the Air Force Cross and twice received the Queen's Commendation for Valuable Service in the Air.

Charles Jackson "Bud" Wildman (born June 3, 1946) is a Canadian politician. He served in the Legislative Assembly of Ontario as a New Democratic Party Member of Provincial Parliament (MPP) from 1975 to 1999, representing the riding of Algoma, and was a cabinet minister in the government of Bob Rae.

Curtis Knight and the Squires were a New York band that was fronted by singer and guitarist Curtis Knight in the mid-1960s. Both Jimi Hendrix and sax player Lonnie Youngblood were members for a while.
Paulus Vallius (Paolo Valla, Paulus Valla, Paulus de la Valle, Paulus de Valle) (1561-1622) was an Italian Jesuit logician.

John Hughes (1814 – 17 June 1889) was a Welsh engineer and businessman who founded the city of Donetsk (then called *Hughesovka*) in present-day Ukraine. After establishing himself in Britain's iron and shipbuilding industries, he moved to Russia in 1870, where he built a major metallurgical plant. The settlement around it grew into a major industrial center, later renamed Donetsk. Hughes remained influential in the region until his death.

Peterson v Minister of Safety and Security is an important case in South African criminal law. For the appellant appeared J Whitehead SC, instructed by JL Martinson & Company, Cape Town; for the respondents, A Schippers SC and S O'Brien, instructed by the State Attorney, Cape Town.

Uckfield Town Council governs the five wards of Uckfield town. The council appoints one councillor who is then known as the Mayor of Uckfield. The current Mayor of Uckfield is Councillor Jackie Love.

Uckfield town council comprises five wards – North, East, West, New Town, and Ridgewood – electing between two and four town councillors each.

Clockwise, from the north, it borders the communities of Maresfield, Buxted, Framfield and Little Horsted.

Taptu was a social media and technology company that built platforms, tools and applications that enabled content on touch screen mobile devices, including phones running iOS and Android. Taptu was a privately held company that was founded in Cambridge in 2007 and was funded by DFJ Esprit and Sofinnova. The company was based in Cambridge and Denver, Colorado.

In September 2012, Taptu was acquired by Mediafed Ltd. The Taptu service shut down March 31, 2015, a year before Mediafed went into administration.
The former Roman Catholic Diocese of Ypres, in present-day Belgium, existed from 1559 to 1801. Its seat was Saint Martin's Cathedral in Ypres. In 1969 it was reconstituted as a titular see.

Klagenfurt Athletic Sports Club or EC KAC (Lang-de) is a professional ice hockey team in the ICE Hockey League. The team plays their home games in Klagenfurt, Carinthia, Austria at Stadthalle Klagenfurt. EC KAC has won the most Austrian ice hockey Championships, a total of 32 times including 11 consecutive titles from 1964 to 1974, and four consecutive from 1985 to 1988. Most recently they won it in 2021.
Frank Simmons Leavitt (June 30, 1891 – May 29, 1953) was an American professional wrestler of the early 1900s, known by the ring name Man Mountain Dean.

Pierre Marinovitch (Lang-sr; 1 August 1898 – 2 October 1919) was a French World War I flying ace credited with 21 confirmed and 3 probable aerial victories. He was the youngest French flying ace of the war, scoring his 5th victory at age 19 years and 169 days. He was killed in an air accident following the war.

The A5 motorway (Greek: *Αυτοκινητόδρομος 5*), also known as Ionia Odos (Ιονία Οδός), is a major highway in Greece, forming part of the European routes E55 and E951. Spanning 196 km (122 mi), it connects Ioannina in the north with Rio in the south, passing through the regions of Epirus, West Greece, and the Peloponnese. The motorway includes the Rio–Antirrio bridge, the world's longest fully suspended cable-stayed bridge, which opened in 2004.  

Key sections include the Agrinio and Arta bypasses, completed in 2009 and 2011 respectively, as well as segments near Amfilochia, Filippiada, and Klokova, which were opened between 2016 and 2017. The project faced delays due to financial issues, expropriation disputes, and challenging terrain. Construction and maintenance of the northern section were awarded to the Nea Odos consortium, while the Patras bypass—the first segment completed—was managed by the Olympia Odos consortium.  

The A5 significantly improves transportation in western Greece, reducing travel times and bypassing urban centers like Agrinio, Arta, and Patras.

Thrilling is an American online marketplace specializing in vintage and secondhand clothing, founded in 2018 by Shilla Kim-Parker and Brad Mallow. Headquartered in Los Angeles, California, the company partners with brick-and-mortar thrift and vintage stores, allowing them to list inventory on its platform while handling sales, shipping, and returns for a 10% commission. Unlike competitors such as Etsy, Depop, and Poshmark, Thrilling focuses exclusively on established physical retailers rather than peer-to-peer sellers.  

The company has raised multiple funding rounds, including $100,000 in seed funding (2018), $2.1 million (2019), and $8.5 million in a Series A round led by Prelude Ventures (2021). Its platform includes tools to help stores digitize inventory, such as AI-assisted pricing and size detection. During the COVID-19 pandemic, Thrilling waived commissions temporarily to support struggling retailers.

ECRM (electronic customer relationship management) is a digital approach to managing customer relationships using internet-based technologies. It builds on traditional CRM by leveraging IT tools to enhance customer interactions, personalize marketing, and improve service efficiency. Key aspects include web-based communication, cloud solutions, mobile CRM (mCRM), and virtual engagement (vCRM). ECRM aims to increase customer loyalty, retention, and satisfaction while reducing costs. Challenges include privacy concerns and implementation failures, but advancements in cloud computing and mobile technology continue to expand its capabilities.

Entropy estimation involves approximating the entropy of a probability distribution from observed data. Common methods include the histogram estimator, which discretizes data into bins but may introduce bias, and sample-spacing techniques, useful for one-dimensional data by analyzing gaps between ordered values. Nearest-neighbor methods estimate entropy based on distances between data points. Bayesian approaches, like the NSB estimator, incorporate prior knowledge for under-sampled data. Another method compares expected entropy with sample entropy, particularly effective for Markov chains. Each technique has strengths depending on data dimensionality and distribution.

Monophysitism is a Christological doctrine asserting that Jesus Christ has only one divine nature, rather than two distinct natures (divine and human) as upheld by the Council of Chalcedon (451). Emerging in the 5th century, it opposed Dyophysitism and was associated with Miaphysite churches, though many later distanced themselves from extreme interpretations. Monophysitism influenced theological and political conflicts in the Byzantine Empire, leading to schisms and imperial attempts at compromise.

Alan Edgar Bristow, OBE, FRAeS (3 September 1923 – 26 April 2009) founded one of the world's largest helicopter service companies, Bristow Helicopters Ltd, which prospered primarily in the international oil and mineral exploration and extraction industries, but also spread into search and rescue, peacekeeping and other fields.

Mariano Ezequiel Arcioni (born 2 April 1970) is an Argentine politician who has served as the governor of Chubut Province since 1 November 2017. From 2015 to 2017, he was the province's vice governor under Mario Das Neves, whose office Arcioni took upon Das Neves's death on 31 October 2017.

Ghatak: Lethal is a 1996 Indian Hindi-language action drama film directed and produced by Rajkumar Santoshi. Starring Sunny Deol, Meenakshi Seshadri, Amrish Puri, and Danny Denzongpa, the film follows Kashi (Deol), a wrestler who takes on a ruthless gangster (Denzongpa) terrorizing a Mumbai slum. The film features music by R. D. Burman and Anu Malik, with a score by Vanraj Bhatia. Made on a budget of ₹6.25 crore, it grossed ₹44 crore at the box office, becoming a commercial success. Originally, Kamal Haasan was cast in the lead role but later withdrew. The film is noted for its intense action sequences and emotional drama.

Hire Benakal is a prehistoric megalithic site located in Koppal district, Karnataka, India. Dating back to the Iron Age (800 BCE–200 CE), it contains around 400 funerary monuments, including dolmens, menhirs, and stone circles, spread across a rocky hill range near the Tungabhadra River. The site features unique structures such as port-holed chambers, anthropomorphic tombs, and cave paintings depicting hunting scenes and animals. A notable feature is a hemispherical boulder that produces a drum-like sound when struck.  

First documented in 1835 by British officer Philip Meadows Taylor, the site was later studied by archaeologists such as Mortimer Wheeler and Adiga Sundara. Recent research by Andrew Bauer has identified over 1,000 antiquities within a 20-hectare area. Despite its historical significance, Hire Benakal remains poorly maintained and receives few visitors. The Archaeological Survey of India oversees the site, though preservation efforts are minimal. Local folklore holds that gods traverse the hills during an annual festival, discouraging cattle grazing in the area.

Mordaunt Fenwick Bisset (1825 – 7 July 1884) of Bagborough in Somerset, 22nd Scottish feudal baron of Lessendrum in Aberdeen, Scotland, was a British Conservative Party Member of Parliament and a famous west-country Master of Staghounds.

The 2012 West Oxfordshire District Council election took place on 3 May 2012 to elect members of West Oxfordshire District Council in Oxfordshire, England. One third of the council was up for election and the Conservative Party stayed in overall control of the council.

After the election, the composition of the council was:

Hurdis Ravenshaw (June 1869 – 6 June 1920) was a British Army officer who served in colonial campaigns and the First World War. Commissioned in 1888, he fought in India's Chitral and Tirah expeditions, the Second Boer War, and commanded the Connaught Rangers in France (1914–15). Promoted to major general, he led the 83rd Brigade and later the 27th Division in Salonika before being captured in 1916 when his ship was sunk. After his release in 1918, he became commander of British forces in South Africa. He disappeared and was found dead in 1920 near Port Elizabeth.

Melchior is traditionally regarded as one of the Three Magi (or Three Kings) who visited the infant Jesus, bearing gifts of gold to symbolize his kingship. Described in medieval tradition as an elderly King of Persia, he is venerated in various Christian denominations. His relics are said to rest in the Shrine of the Three Kings at Cologne Cathedral. Melchior is commemorated on Epiphany (6 January) and is a patron saint of travelers, merchants, and Cologne, Germany.

Hilary Joseph Francis Lofting (23 May 1881 – 3 May 1939) was an Australian novelist, travel writer, journalist and editor.

He was the eldest brother of Hugh Lofting (1886–1947), author of Doctor Dolittle.

The Manned Spaceflight Engineer Program (MSE) was a U.S. Department of Defense initiative (1979–1988) to train military astronauts for classified Space Shuttle missions. Only two MSEs flew before the program ended due to the *Challenger* disaster and a shift to unmanned rockets.
Larissa Takchi (born 1997) is an Australian cook who won the eleventh series of MasterChef Australia in 2019. At 22 years old, to date she was the youngest contestant to win the competition.

Peredur (IPA-cy, Old Welsh Peretur) is the name of a number of men from the boundaries of history and legend in sub-Roman Britain. The Peredur who is most familiar to a modern audience is the character who made his entrance as a knight in the Arthurian world of Middle Welsh prose literature.
Year of the Gun is a 1991 American thriller film directed by John Frankenheimer and starring Andrew McCarthy, Sharon Stone and Valeria Golino.

The Century Plaza Hotel, now known as the Fairmont Century Plaza, is a historic luxury hotel in Century City, Los Angeles, California. Designed by architect Minoru Yamasaki and opened in 1966, it was a centerpiece of the Century City development. The hotel has hosted numerous high-profile events, including political fundraisers, state dinners, and awards ceremonies. Presidents Lyndon B. Johnson and Ronald Reagan frequently used it, with Reagan dubbing it his "Western White House." After a $2.5 billion renovation, it reopened in 2021 under Fairmont Hotels and Resorts, preserving its iconic curved design while adding residential towers. The hotel has also appeared in films and video games.

An extraocular implant (also known as eyeball jewelry) is a cosmetic implant involving a tiny piece of decorative jewelry which is implanted within the superficial, interpalpebral conjunctiva or sclera of the human eye.

Nintendo PowerFest '94 was a 1994 Nintendo-promoted video game competition. Most of the town competitions were held in customized trailers outside of department stores. It was based on scoring points in a special Game Pak. The three Super Nintendo Entertainment System games (Super Mario Bros.: The Lost Levels, Super Mario Kart, and Ken Griffey, Jr. Presents Major League Baseball) were played within a time limit of six minutes.

The First Congregational Church and Parsonage is a historic church complex at 23 Pepperrell Road in Kittery Point, Maine. Established in 1653, the current Greek Revival church was built in 1729 after its predecessor burned down. The adjacent 1733 burial ground contains graves of prominent local figures, including members of the Pepperrell family. The church was moved and renovated in 1840, while the original parsonage (later a community center) was replaced in 1910. The site was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1978, with a boundary expansion in 1997.

Edith Guerrier (1870–1958) was a pioneer in the field of library science. Guerrier is best known for developing progressive library programs in the 1890s, including a reading program and a pottery studio for girls of Boston's North End, an urban immigrant center during the Progressive Era.

The 1957 Alberta liquor plebiscite was a province-wide plebiscite conducted in Alberta, Canada The plebiscite asked voters if they were in favour of adding extra ALCB outlets in their district. It was held on October 30, 1957. At the time, the Alberta Liquor Control Board operated only a few stores from which liquor could be legally purchased.

A vote among egg producers was also held on the question of an egg marketing board.

An additional vote was held regarding mixed drinking in the major cities, also on October 30, 1957.

The 1957 votes were not held in conjunction with an Alberta election.

Future of the Left is a Welsh alternative rock band formed in 2005 by ex-members of Mclusky and Jarcrew. Known for their noisy, post-hardcore sound, the band has released five studio albums, including *Curses* (2007) and *The Plot Against Common Sense* (2012). They have undergone several lineup changes and have crowdfunded later albums.

Menodora is a genus of perennial plants and shrubs in the olive family Oleaceae. Its 23 species (as per Green 2003) are found in the temperate Americas and in southern Africa. These are uniform species of deserts and arid grasslands or savannas.

In mathematics, the dimension of a vector space V is the cardinality (i.e., the number of vectors) of a basis of V over its base field. It is sometimes called Hamel dimension (after Georg Hamel) or algebraic dimension to distinguish it from other types of dimension.

For every vector space there exists a basis, and all bases of a vector space have equal cardinality; as a result, the dimension of a vector space is uniquely defined. We say is finite-dimensional if the dimension of is finite, and infinite-dimensional if its dimension is infinite.

The dimension of the vector space over the field can be written as or as read "dimension of over". When can be inferred from context, is typically written.
The Light Helicopter Experimental (LHX) was a U.S. Army program to develop a next-generation reconnaissance and attack helicopter, culminating in the RAH-66 Comanche before its cancellation in 2004.

Miniclip is a Swiss-based online gaming platform and developer, founded on 30 March 2001 and owned by Tencent since 2015. It offers a variety of free-to-play games across multiple platforms, including mobile (iOS, Android), PC, and consoles (Xbox, PlayStation). Known for popular titles such as *8 Ball Pool*, *Agar.io*, and *Golf Battle*, Miniclip expanded its reach by distributing games on Xbox for Windows 8 and later released *MX Nitro* for Xbox One, PlayStation 4, and PC. The company has faced security concerns in the past, including malware incidents in 2005 and 2006. Headquartered in Neuchâtel, Switzerland, Miniclip remains active, providing games in multiple languages worldwide.

Victor de Laprade (1812–1883) was a French poet, critic, and academic, known for his religious and nature-inspired verse. Elected to the Académie française in 1858, he also served in the National Assembly.

Transbaikal, Trans-Baikal, Transbaikalia (Russian: Забайка́лье, romanized: Zabaykalye, IPA: [zəbɐjˈkaljjɪ]), or Dauria (Даурия, Dauriya) is a mountainous region to the east of or "beyond" (trans-) Lake Baikal in Far Eastern Russia.

The steppe and wetland landscapes of Dauria are protected by the Daurian Nature Reserve, which forms part of a World Heritage Site named "The Landscapes of Dauria".

Maria Clara Eimmart (1676–1707) was a German astronomer and scientific illustrator known for her detailed drawings of the moon's phases and a 1706 solar eclipse. She was the daughter of astronomer Georg Christoph Eimmart the Younger and assisted her father and later her husband in their astronomical work.

Ecological sanitation, commonly abbreviated as ecosan (also spelled eco-san or EcoSan), is an approach to sanitation provision which aims to safely reuse excreta in agriculture. It is an approach, rather than a technology or a device which is characterized by a desire to "close the loop", mainly for the nutrients and organic matter between sanitation and agriculture in a safe manner. One of the aims is to minimise the use of non-renewable resources. When properly designed and operated, ecosan systems provide a hygienically safe system to convert human excreta into nutrients to be returned to the soil, and water to be returned to the land. Ecosan is also called resource-oriented sanitation.

Hilary Joseph Francis Lofting (23 May 1881 – 3 May 1939) was an English-born Australian novelist, travel writer, and journalist. Writing under the pseudonym Francis Brien, he co-authored *The Happy Vagabond* (1928) and *Bail Up!* (1939) with his wife, Margaret Fane.

Ian Simmonds (born 1966), also known as Juryman, is a Welsh-born electronic musician. He is one of a few talents "to successfully breach the chasm that keeps DJ culture from more tangible, organic realms." His complex musical landscapes are often accompanied by his deep calm voice, making poetic, political and social statements at the same time. He is a self-taught musician, singing and playing trumpet, bass guitar, piano and is a producer with numerous releases, contributions and co-productions.

Mieczyslaw "Mickey Doyle" Kuzik is a fictional character in the HBO TV series Boardwalk Empire. He is played by Paul Sparks. Mickey Doyle is loosely based on Polish American mobster Mickey Duffy.

Mickey is a gangster in Prohibition-era Atlantic City. For the first two seasons, he is the main rival of harlem liquor kingpin Chalky White. He is known for his distinctive, nasal laugh, and serves as the series' comic relief.

Collar × Malice is a visual novel video game developed and published by Idea Factory under their Otomate brand. It was released for PlayStation Vita on August 18, 2016, in Japan, and by Aksys Games in North America and Europe on July 28, 2017. A Nintendo Switch port was also released in 2020. The game was well received by critics, and sold well in Japan for a niche game of its genre and platform. A fan disc, Collar × Malice: Unlimited, was released on July 26, 2018. An anime film adaptation is set to premiere in 2023.

A Walk in the Sun is a 1945 American war film based on the novel by Harry Brown, who was a writer for Yank, the Army Weekly based in England. The book was serialized in Liberty Magazine in October 1944.

The film was directed by Lewis Milestone, stars Dana Andrews and features Richard Conte, George Tyne, John Ireland, Lloyd Bridges, Sterling Holloway, Norman Lloyd, Herbert Rudley and Richard Benedict, with narration by Burgess Meredith.

In 2016, the film was deemed "culturally, historically, or aesthetically significant" by the United States Library of Congress, and selected for preservation in its National Film Registry.
